From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Federal region of Belgium including the capital

This article is about the Brussels-Capital Region. For the municipality within it, which is the de jure national capital, see [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"). For other places, see [Brussels (disambiguation)](/wiki/Brussels_(disambiguation) "Brussels (disambiguation)").

"Bruxelles" redirects here. For the Canadian community, see [Bruxelles, Manitoba](/wiki/Bruxelles,_Manitoba "Bruxelles, Manitoba").

**Brussels**,[[a]](#cite_note-fn1-12) officially the **Brussels-Capital Region**,[[b]](#cite_note-fn2-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Belgian_Constitution-14)[[13]](#cite_note-CIRB-creation-15) is a [region](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium#Regions "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") comprising [19 municipalities](#Municipalities), including the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"), which is the capital of Belgium.[[14]](#cite_note-article_194-16) The Brussels-Capital Region is located in the central portion of the country. It is a part of both the [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium")[[15]](#cite_note-décret-17) and the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community"),[[16]](#cite_note-18) and is separate from the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") (Flanders), within which it forms an [enclave](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave"), and the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region") (Wallonia), located less than 4 km (2.5 mi) south.[[17]](#cite_note-decreet-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20)

Brussels grew from a small rural settlement on the river [Senne](/wiki/Senne_(river) "Senne (river)") to become an important city-region in Europe. Since the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), it has been a major centre for [international politics](/wiki/International_politics "International politics") and home to numerous international organisations, politicians, [diplomats](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy") and [civil servants](/wiki/Civil_servant "Civil servant").[[19]](#cite_note-BBC_Belgium-21) Brussels is the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* capital of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), as it hosts a number of principal [EU institutions](/wiki/EU_institutions "EU institutions"), including its [administrative-legislative](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission"), [executive-political](/wiki/European_Council "European Council"), and [legislative](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") branches (though the judicial branch is located in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City"), and the European Parliament meets for a minority of the year in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg")).[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey2007-1)[[20]](#cite_note-12010E/PRO/06-22)[[c]](#cite_note-23) Because of this, its name is sometimes used [metonymically](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") to describe the EU and its institutions.[[21]](#cite_note-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) The secretariat of the [Benelux](/wiki/Benelux "Benelux") and the [headquarters](/wiki/NATO_Headquarters "NATO Headquarters") of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") are also located in Brussels.[[23]](#cite_note-26)[[24]](#cite_note-27)

Brussels is the most densely populated region in Belgium, and although it has the highest [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita"),[[25]](#cite_note-:4-28) it has the lowest available income per household.[[26]](#cite_note-statbel-29) The Brussels Region covers 162 km2 (63 sq mi) and has a population of over 1.2 million.[[27]](#cite_note-:8-30) Its five times larger [metropolitan area](/wiki/Brussels_metropolitan_area "Brussels metropolitan area") comprises over 2.5 million people, which makes it the [largest in Belgium](/wiki/Metropolitan_areas_in_Belgium "Metropolitan areas in Belgium").[[28]](#cite_note-BelMun2008-31)[[29]](#cite_note-BelMetr-32)[[30]](#cite_note-:5-33) It is also part of a large [diamond-shaped conurbation](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond"), extending towards the cities of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), as well as the province of [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant"), in total home to over 5 million people.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_MeeterenBoussauwDerudderWitlox2016-34) As Belgium's economic capital and a top financial centre in Western Europe with [Euronext Brussels](/wiki/Euronext_Brussels "Euronext Brussels"), Brussels is classified as an *Alpha* [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[32]](#cite_note-35) It is also a national and international hub for rail, road and air traffic,[[33]](#cite_note-36) and is sometimes considered, together with Belgium, as Europe's geographic, economic and cultural crossroads.[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39) The [Brussels Metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") is the only [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in Belgium. In addition, both its [airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") and [railway stations](/wiki/List_of_railway_stations_in_Belgium "List of railway stations in Belgium") are the largest and busiest in the country.[[37]](#cite_note-:2-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41)

Historically Dutch-speaking, Brussels saw a [language shift to French](/wiki/Francization_of_Brussels "Francization of Brussels") from the late 19th century.[[39]](#cite_note-42) Since its creation in 1989, the Brussels-Capital Region has been officially bilingual in French and Dutch,[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44) although French is the majority language and *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*.[[42]](#cite_note-BruS13EN-45) Brussels is also increasingly becoming multilingual. English is spoken widely and many migrants and [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate") speak other languages as well.[[42]](#cite_note-BruS13EN-45)[[43]](#cite_note-:7-46)

Brussels is known for its cuisine and gastronomic offer (including its local [waffle](/wiki/Belgian_waffle "Belgian waffle"), its [chocolate](/wiki/Belgian_chocolate "Belgian chocolate"), its [French fries](/wiki/French_fries "French fries") and its numerous types of [beers](/wiki/Beer_in_Belgium "Beer in Belgium")),[[44]](#cite_note-47) as well as its historical and architectural landmarks; some of them are registered as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[45]](#cite_note-48) Principal attractions include its historic [Grand-Place/Grote Markt](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place") (main square), *[Manneken Pis](/wiki/Manneken_Pis "Manneken Pis")*, the [Atomium](/wiki/Atomium "Atomium"), and cultural institutions such as [La Monnaie/De Munt](/wiki/La_Monnaie/De_Munt "La Monnaie/De Munt") and the [Museums of Art and History](/wiki/Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History "Royal Museums of Art and History"). Due to its long tradition of [Belgian comics](/wiki/Belgian_comics "Belgian comics"), Brussels is also hailed as a capital of the [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip").[[3]](#cite_note-Comic-3)[[46]](#cite_note-:1-49)

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

See also: [Names of European cities in different languages (B)](/wiki/Names_of_European_cities_in_different_languages_(B) "Names of European cities in different languages (B)")

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The most common theory of the origin of the name *Brussels* is that it derives from the [Old Dutch](/wiki/Old_Dutch "Old Dutch") *Bruocsella*, *Broekzele* or *Broeksel*, meaning 'marsh' (*bruoc* / *broek*) and 'home, settlement' (*sella* / *zele* / *sel*) or 'settlement in the marsh'.[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-vgc-51) Saint [Vindicianus](/wiki/Vindicianus "Vindicianus"), the Bishop of [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai"), made the first recorded reference to the place *Brosella* in 695,[[49]](#cite_note-52) when it was still a [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(place) "Hamlet (place)"). The names of all the municipalities in the Brussels-Capital Region are also of Dutch origin, except for [Evere](/wiki/Evere "Evere"), which is possibly [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") or [Old Frankish](/wiki/Old_Frankish "Old Frankish").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETribotDefawe20063-53)

### Pronunciation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pronunciation")]

In French, *Bruxelles* is pronounced [[bʁysɛl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Bruxelles.ogg "File:Fr-Bruxelles.ogg") (the *x* is pronounced [/s/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") and the final *s* is silent) and in Dutch, *Brussel* is pronounced [[ˈbrʏsəl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Brussel.ogg "File:Nl-Brussel.ogg"). Inhabitants of Brussels are known in French as *Bruxellois* (pronounced [[bʁysɛlwa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-bruxellois.ogg "File:Fr-bruxellois.ogg")) and in Dutch as *Brusselaars* (pronounced [[ˈbrʏsəlaːrs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")). In the [Brabantian dialect](/wiki/Brabantian_dialect "Brabantian dialect") of Brussels (known as [Brusselian](/wiki/Brusselian_dialect "Brusselian dialect"), and also sometimes referred to as [Marollian](/wiki/Marollian_dialect "Marollian dialect")),[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller199425-54) they are called *Brusseleers* or *Brusseleirs*.[[52]](#cite_note-evans-55)

Originally, the written *x* was pronounced [/ks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"). In the [Belgian French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") pronunciation as well as in Dutch, the *k* eventually disappeared, as reflected in the current [Dutch spelling](/wiki/Dutch_spelling "Dutch spelling"), whereas in the more conservative [French form](/wiki/French_orthography "French orthography"), the spelling remained.[[53]](#cite_note-56) The pronunciation [/ks/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") in French only dates from the 18th century,[*[contradictory](/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles "Category:All self-contradictory articles")*] but this modification did not affect the traditional Brussels usage. In France, the pronunciations [[bʁyksɛl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Bruxelles.oga "File:Fr-Bruxelles.oga") and [[bʁyksɛlwa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") (for *bruxellois*) are often heard, but are rather rare in Belgium.[[54]](#cite_note-Lerond,_1980_pp._477-57)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Brussels](/wiki/History_of_Brussels "History of Brussels")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Brussels](/wiki/Timeline_of_Brussels "Timeline of Brussels").

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Charles_de_France_fondateur_de_Bruxelles_976_MOD.jpg/250px-Charles_de_France_fondateur_de_Bruxelles_976_MOD.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_de_France_fondateur_de_Bruxelles_976_MOD.jpg)

[Charles of Lorraine](/wiki/Charles,_Duke_of_Lower_Lorraine "Charles, Duke of Lower Lorraine"), traditionally considered the founder of what would become Brussels, c. 979

The history of Brussels is closely linked to that of [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"). Traces of human settlement go back to the [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age"), with vestiges and place-names related to the civilisation of [megaliths](/wiki/Megalith "Megalith"), [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmen "Dolmen") and [standing stones](/wiki/Standing_stones "Standing stones") (Plattesteen near the [Grand-Place/Grote Markt](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place") and [Tomberg](/wiki/Tomberg_metro_station "Tomberg metro station") in [Woluwe-Saint-Lambert](/wiki/Woluwe-Saint-Lambert "Woluwe-Saint-Lambert"), for example). During [late antiquity](/wiki/Late_antiquity "Late antiquity"), the region was home to [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") occupation, as attested by archaeological evidence discovered on the current site of [Tour & Taxis](/wiki/Tour_%26_Taxis "Tour & Taxis"), north-west of the [Pentagon](/wiki/Pentagon_(Brussels) "Pentagon (Brussels)") (Brussels' city centre).[[55]](#cite_note-58)[[56]](#cite_note-59) Following the decline of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), it was incorporated into the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire").

According to local legend, the origin of the settlement that was to become Brussels lies in Saint [Gaugericus](/wiki/Gaugericus "Gaugericus")' construction of a chapel on [an island](/wiki/Saint-G%C3%A9ry_Island "Saint-Géry Island") in the river [Senne](/wiki/Senne_(river) "Senne (river)") around 580.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEState2004269-60) The official founding of Brussels is usually said to be around 979, when [Charles, Duke of Lower Lorraine](/wiki/Charles,_Duke_of_Lower_Lorraine "Charles, Duke of Lower Lorraine"), transferred the [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") of the [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") Saint [Gudula](/wiki/Gudula "Gudula") from [Moorsel](/wiki/Moorsel "Moorsel") (located in today's province of [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders")) to Saint Gaugericus' chapel. When [Otto II, Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Otto_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Otto II, Holy Roman Emperor"), appointed the same Charles to become Duke of [Lower Lotharingia](/wiki/Lower_Lotharingia "Lower Lotharingia") in 977,[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiché1983276-61) Charles ordered the construction of the city's first permanent fortification, doing so on that same island.

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[Lambert I, Count of Louvain](/wiki/Lambert_I,_Count_of_Louvain "Lambert I, Count of Louvain"), gained the County of Brussels around 1000 by marrying Charles' daughter. Because of its location on the banks of the Senne, on an [important trade route](/wiki/Brabant_Road "Brabant Road") between the [Flemish](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") cities of [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), and [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") in the [Kingdom of Germany](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Germany "Kingdom of Germany"), Brussels became a commercial centre specialised in the textile trade. The town grew quite rapidly and extended towards the upper town (Treurenberg, [Coudenberg](/wiki/Coudenberg "Coudenberg"), and [Sablon/Zavel](/wiki/Sablon,_Brussels "Sablon, Brussels") areas), where there was a reduced risk of floods. As the town grew to a population of around 30,000, the surrounding marshes were drained to allow for further expansion. In 1183, the Counts of Leuven became [Dukes of Brabant](/wiki/Dukes_of_Brabant "Dukes of Brabant"). Brabant, unlike the county of Flanders, was not fief of the king of France but was incorporated into the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire").

In the early 13th century, the [first walls of Brussels](/wiki/First_walls_of_Brussels "First walls of Brussels") were built[[59]](#cite_note-62) and after this, the city grew significantly. Around this time, work began on what is now the [Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula "Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula") (1225), replacing an older [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") church.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrigode1938185–215-63) To let the city expand, [a second set of walls](/wiki/Second_walls_of_Brussels "Second walls of Brussels") was erected between 1356 and 1383. Traces of these walls can still be seen; the [Small Ring](/wiki/Small_Ring,_Brussels "Small Ring, Brussels"), a series of boulevards bounding the historical city centre, follows their former course.

### Early modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Brussel.1610.a.jpg/250px-Brussel.1610.a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussel.1610.a.jpg)

View of Brussels, c. 1610

In the 14th century, the marriage between heiress [Margaret III, Countess of Flanders](/wiki/Margaret_III,_Countess_of_Flanders "Margaret III, Countess of Flanders"), and [Philip the Bold](/wiki/Philip_the_Bold "Philip the Bold"), [Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Duke_of_Burgundy "Duke of Burgundy"), produced a new Duke of Brabant of the [House of Valois](/wiki/House_of_Valois "House of Valois"), namely [Anthony](/wiki/Anthony,_Duke_of_Brabant "Anthony, Duke of Brabant"), their son.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlockmansPrevenier199930–31-64) In 1477, the Burgundian duke [Charles the Bold](/wiki/Charles_the_Bold "Charles the Bold") perished in the [Battle of Nancy](/wiki/Battle_of_Nancy "Battle of Nancy").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKirk1868542-65) Through the marriage of his daughter [Mary of Burgundy](/wiki/Mary_of_Burgundy "Mary of Burgundy") (who was born in Brussels) to Holy Roman Emperor [Maximilian I](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor"), the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") fell under [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") sovereignty.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArmstrong1957228-66) Brabant was integrated into this composite state, and Brussels flourished as the Princely Capital of the prosperous [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands"), also known as the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces"). After the death of Mary in 1482, her son [Philip the Handsome](/wiki/Philip_the_Handsome "Philip the Handsome") succeeded as Duke of Burgundy and Brabant.

Philip died in 1506, and he was succeeded by his son [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") who then also became [King of Spain](/wiki/King_of_Spain "King of Spain") (crowned in the [Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula "Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula")) and even Holy Roman Emperor at the death of his grandfather Maximilian I in 1519. Charles was now the ruler of a [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_Empire "Habsburg Empire") "on which the sun never sets" with Brussels serving as one of his main capitals.[[2]](#cite_note-Capital-2)[[64]](#cite_note-67) It was in the [Coudenberg Palace](/wiki/Coudenberg_Palace "Coudenberg Palace") that Charles V was declared of age in 1515, and it was there in 1555 that he abdicated all of his possessions and passed the [Habsburg Netherlands](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") to King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasseige19954-68) This palace, famous all over Europe, had greatly expanded since it had first become the seat of the Dukes of Brabant, but it was destroyed by fire in 1731.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMardaga1994222-69)[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasseige19956–7-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Grand-_Place_BXL1695_-01.jpg/250px-Grand-_Place_BXL1695_-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand-_Place_BXL1695_-01.jpg)

The [Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place") after [the 1695 bombardment](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Brussels_(1695) "Bombardment of Brussels (1695)") by the French army

In the 16th and 17th centuries, Brussels was a centre for the [lace](/wiki/Lace "Lace") industry. In addition, [Brussels tapestry](/wiki/Brussels_tapestry "Brussels tapestry") hung on the walls of castles throughout Europe.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-72) In 1695, during the [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War"), King [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") of France sent troops to [bombard Brussels with artillery](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Brussels_(1695) "Bombardment of Brussels (1695)"). Together with the resulting fire, it was the most destructive event in the entire history of Brussels. The Grand-Place was destroyed, along with 4,000 buildings—a third of all the buildings in the city. The reconstruction of [the city centre](/wiki/Pentagon_(Brussels) "Pentagon (Brussels)"), effected during subsequent years, profoundly changed its appearance and left numerous traces still visible today.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECulotHennautDemanetMierop1992-73)

During the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") Brussels fell in Anglo-Dutch hands following the [Battle of Ramillies](/wiki/Battle_of_Ramillies "Battle of Ramillies") in 1706. In 1708, Brussels again sustained a [French attack](/wiki/Assault_on_Brussels_(1708) "Assault on Brussels (1708)"), which it repelled. Following the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") in 1713, Spanish sovereignty over the Southern Netherlands was transferred to the Austrian branch of the House of Habsburg. This event started the era of the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands"). Brussels [was captured](/wiki/Siege_of_Brussels "Siege of Brussels") by France in 1746, during the [War of the Austrian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Austrian_Succession "War of the Austrian Succession"),[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2009753-74) but was handed back to Austria three years later. It remained with Austria until 1795, when the Southern Netherlands were captured and annexed by France, and the city became the [chef-lieu](/wiki/Prefectures_in_France "Prefectures in France") of the [department of the Dyle](/wiki/Dyle_(department) "Dyle (department)").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOudiette1804XIII-75)[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuvergier1835300-76) The French rule ended in 1815, with the defeat of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") on the [battlefield of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"), located south of today's Brussels-Capital Region.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalloyHayt200686–90-77) With the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna"), the Southern Netherlands joined the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands"), under King [William I of Orange](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands"). The former Dyle department became the province of [South Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant#United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "Province of Brabant"), with Brussels as its capital.

### Late modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Late modern")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Wappers_-_Episodes_from_September_Days_1830_on_the_Place_de_l%E2%80%99H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_in_Brussels.JPG/250px-Wappers_-_Episodes_from_September_Days_1830_on_the_Place_de_l%E2%80%99H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_in_Brussels.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wappers_-_Episodes_from_September_Days_1830_on_the_Place_de_l%E2%80%99H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville_in_Brussels.JPG)

*Episode of the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") of 1830*, [Gustaf Wappers](/wiki/Gustaf_Wappers "Gustaf Wappers"), 1834

In 1830, the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") began in Brussels, after a performance of [Auber's](/wiki/Daniel-Francois-Esprit_Auber "Daniel-Francois-Esprit Auber") opera *[La Muette de Portici](/wiki/La_Muette_de_Portici "La Muette de Portici")* at the [Royal Theatre of La Monnaie](/wiki/La_Monnaie "La Monnaie").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlatin197953–54-78) The city became the capital and seat of government of the new nation. South Brabant was renamed simply [Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant"), with Brussels as its administrative centre. On 21 July 1831, [Leopold I](/wiki/Leopold_I_of_Belgium "Leopold I of Belgium"), the first [King of the Belgians](/wiki/King_of_the_Belgians "King of the Belgians"), ascended the throne,[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPirenne194830-79) undertaking the destruction of the city walls and the construction of many buildings.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey200996-80)

Following independence, Brussels underwent many more changes. It became a financial centre, thanks to the dozens of companies launched by the *[Société Générale de Belgique](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_G%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale_de_Belgique "Société Générale de Belgique")*. The [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") and the opening of the [Brussels–Charleroi Canal](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Charleroi_Canal "Brussels–Charleroi Canal") in 1832 brought prosperity to the city through commerce and manufacturing.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECharruadas2005-81)[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey200996–97-82) The [Free University of Brussels](/wiki/Free_University_of_Brussels_(1834%E2%80%931969) "Free University of Brussels (1834–1969)") was established in 1834 and [Saint-Louis University](/wiki/Saint-Louis_University,_Brussels "Saint-Louis University, Brussels") in 1858. In 1835, the [first passenger railway](/wiki/History_of_rail_transport_in_Belgium "History of rail transport in Belgium") built outside England linked the municipality of [Molenbeek-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Molenbeek-Saint-Jean "Molenbeek-Saint-Jean") with [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWolmar201018–20-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Bruxelles%2C_Place_Royale%2C_-Brussels%2C_Belgium--LCCN2001697909.jpg/250px-Bruxelles%2C_Place_Royale%2C_-Brussels%2C_Belgium--LCCN2001697909.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruxelles,_Place_Royale,_-Brussels,_Belgium--LCCN2001697909.jpg)

The [Place Royale/Koningsplein](/wiki/Place_Royale,_Brussels "Place Royale, Brussels") in the late 19th century

During the 19th century, the population of Brussels grew considerably; from about 80,000 to more than 625,000 people for the city and its surroundings. The Senne had become a serious [health hazard](/wiki/Health_hazard "Health hazard"), and from 1867 to 1871, under the tenure of the [city's then-mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_City_of_Brussels "Mayor of the City of Brussels"), [Jules Anspach](/wiki/Jules_Anspach "Jules Anspach"), its entire course through the urban area was [completely covered over](/wiki/Covering_of_the_Senne "Covering of the Senne").[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey199065-84) This allowed [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") and the construction of modern buildings of *[Haussmann](/wiki/Haussmann%27s_renovation_of_Paris "Haussmann's renovation of Paris")-esque* style along grand [central boulevards](/wiki/Central_Boulevards_of_Brussels "Central Boulevards of Brussels"), characteristic of downtown Brussels today.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEggericx19975-85) Buildings such as the [Bourse Palace](/wiki/Bourse_Palace "Bourse Palace") (1873), the [Palace of Justice](/wiki/Palace_of_Justice,_Brussels "Palace of Justice, Brussels") (1883) and [Saint Mary's Royal Church](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_Royal_Church "Saint Mary's Royal Church") (1885) date from this period. This development continued throughout the reign of King [Leopold II](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium"). The [International Exposition of 1897](/wiki/Brussels_International_Exposition_(1897) "Brussels International Exposition (1897)") contributed to the promotion of the infrastructure.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchroeder-GudehusRasmussen1992-86) Among other things, the [Palace of the Colonies](/wiki/Africa_Palace "Africa Palace"), today's [Royal Museum for Central Africa](/wiki/Royal_Museum_for_Central_Africa "Royal Museum for Central Africa"), in the suburb of [Tervuren](/wiki/Tervuren "Tervuren"), was connected to the capital by the construction of an [11 km-long (6.8 mi) grand alley](/wiki/Avenue_de_Tervueren "Avenue de Tervueren").

Brussels was one of the major European centres of the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau_in_Brussels "Art Nouveau in Brussels") style in the 1890s and early 1900s.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECulotPirlot2005-87) The architects [Victor Horta](/wiki/Victor_Horta "Victor Horta"), [Paul Hankar](/wiki/Paul_Hankar "Paul Hankar"), and [Henry van de Velde](/wiki/Henry_van_de_Velde "Henry van de Velde"), among others, were known for their designs, many of which survive today.[[85]](#cite_note-:6-88)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Solvay_conference_1927.jpg/250px-Solvay_conference_1927.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solvay_conference_1927.jpg)

The 1927 [Solvay Conference](/wiki/Solvay_Conference "Solvay Conference") in Brussels was the fifth world physics conference.

During the 20th century, the city hosted various fairs and conferences, including the [Solvay Conference](/wiki/Solvay_Conference "Solvay Conference") on Physics and on Chemistry, and three [world's fairs](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair"): the [Brussels International Exposition of 1910](/wiki/Brussels_International_Exposition_(1910) "Brussels International Exposition (1910)"), the [Brussels International Exposition of 1935](/wiki/Brussels_International_Exposition_of_1935 "Brussels International Exposition of 1935") and the 1958 Brussels World's Fair ([Expo 58](/wiki/Expo_58 "Expo 58")).[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchroeder-GudehusRasmussen1992-86) During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Brussels was an [occupied](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Belgium_during_World_War_I "German occupation of Belgium during World War I") city. It escaped large-scale destruction,[[86]](#cite_note-89) although the [interwar period](/wiki/Interwar_period "Interwar period") would still see a significant amount of (re)construction.[[d]](#cite_note-90)[[87]](#cite_note-91) In November 1918, after the [German Revolution](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919") had broken out, Brussels was embroiled in street battles between revolutionary soldiers who wanted to end the occupation and their imperialist counterparts; additionally, explosives left behind by retreating German troops damaged infrastructure and around 2,300 houses.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Brussels was [occupied by German forces](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Belgium_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Belgium during World War II") once again. While it was again spared major damage, a deadly bombing took place on 7 September 1943. It was carried out by the [American airforce](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"), which had tried to aim at [SABCA](/wiki/SABCA "SABCA") facilities in [Haren](/wiki/Haren,_Belgium "Haren, Belgium"), but accidentally struck an area in [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles"), killing 282 civilians.[[89]](#cite_note-93) The city was liberated by the British [Guards Armoured Division](/wiki/Guards_Armoured_Division "Guards Armoured Division") on 3 September 1944. [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport"), in the suburb of [Zaventem](/wiki/Zaventem "Zaventem"), traces its roots to [a German military airport](/wiki/Melsbroek_Air_Base "Melsbroek Air Base") that was built in nearby [Melsbroek](/wiki/Steenokkerzeel "Steenokkerzeel").[[90]](#cite_note-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Scenes_of_jubilation_as_British_troops_liberate_Brussels%2C_4_September_1944._BU508.jpg/250px-Scenes_of_jubilation_as_British_troops_liberate_Brussels%2C_4_September_1944._BU508.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scenes_of_jubilation_as_British_troops_liberate_Brussels,_4_September_1944._BU508.jpg)

British tanks [arrive in Brussels](/wiki/Liberation_of_Belgium "Liberation of Belgium") on 4 September 1944, ending the [German occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Belgium_during_World_War_II "German occupation of Belgium during World War II")

After World War II, Brussels underwent extensive modernisation. The construction of the [North–South connection](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_connection "North–South connection"), linking the main railway stations in the city, was completed in 1952, while the first *[premetro](/wiki/Brussels_premetro "Brussels premetro")* (underground tram) service was launched in 1969,[[91]](#cite_note-1960s-95) and the first [Metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") line was opened in 1976.[[92]](#cite_note-1970s-96) Starting from the early 1960s, Brussels became the *de facto* capital of what would become the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU), and many modern offices were built. Development was allowed to proceed with little regard to the aesthetics of newer buildings, and numerous architectural landmarks were demolished to make way for newer buildings that often clashed with their surroundings, giving name to the process of [Brusselisation](/wiki/Brusselisation "Brusselisation").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEState200451–52-97)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStubbsMakaš2011121-98)

### Contemporary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Contemporary")]

The Brussels-Capital Region was formed on 18 June 1989, after a constitutional reform in 1988.[[95]](#cite_note-99) It is one of the three [federal regions of Belgium](/wiki/Federal_regions_of_Belgium "Federal regions of Belgium"), along with [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), and has bilingual status.[[12]](#cite_note-Belgian_Constitution-14)[[13]](#cite_note-CIRB-creation-15) The [yellow iris](/wiki/Yellow_iris "Yellow iris") is the region's emblem (referring to the presence of these flowers on the city's original site) and a stylised version is featured on its official flag.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

In the 21st century, Brussels has become an important venue for international events. In 2000, it was named [European Capital of Culture](/wiki/European_Capital_of_Culture "European Capital of Culture") alongside eight other European cities.[[97]](#cite_note-101) In 2013, the city was the site of the [Brussels Agreement](/wiki/Brussels_Agreement_(2013) "Brussels Agreement (2013)").[[98]](#cite_note-102) In 2014, it hosted the [40th G7 summit](/wiki/40th_G7_summit "40th G7 summit"),[[99]](#cite_note-:0-103) and in 2017, 2018 and 2021 respectively the [28th](/wiki/2017_Brussels_summit "2017 Brussels summit"), [29th](/wiki/2018_Brussels_summit "2018 Brussels summit") and [31st NATO Summits](/wiki/2021_Brussels_summit "2021 Brussels summit").[[100]](#cite_note-104)[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[102]](#cite_note-:9-106)

[On 22 March 2016](/wiki/2016_Brussels_bombings "2016 Brussels bombings"), three coordinated [nail bombings](/wiki/Nail_bomb "Nail bomb") were detonated by [ISIL](/wiki/ISIL "ISIL") in Brussels—two at [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") in [Zaventem](/wiki/Zaventem "Zaventem") and one at [Maalbeek/Maelbeek metro station](/wiki/Maalbeek/Maelbeek_metro_station "Maalbeek/Maelbeek metro station")—resulting in 32 victims and three [suicide bombers](/wiki/Suicide_bombers "Suicide bombers") killed, and 330 people were injured. It was the deadliest act of [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") in Belgium.[[103]](#cite_note-independent-107)[[104]](#cite_note-BBC35869985-108)[[105]](#cite_note-BBC35880119-109)[[106]](#cite_note-Guardian220317-110)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Location and topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Location and topography")]

Further information: [Geography of Belgium](/wiki/Geography_of_Belgium "Geography of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Brussels_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-05-30.jpg/250px-Brussels_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-05-30.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-05-30.jpg)

A 2020 satellite image of the Greater Brussels area

Brussels lies in the north-central part of Belgium, about 110 km (68 mi) from the Belgian coast and about 180 km (110 mi) from the country's southern tip. It is located in the heartland of the Brabantian Plateau, about 45 km (28 mi) south of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") ([Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders")), and 50 km (31 mi) north of [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") ([Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")). Its average [elevation](/wiki/Elevation "Elevation") is 57 m (187 ft) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"), varying from a low point in the valley of the almost completely covered [Senne](/wiki/Senne_(river) "Senne (river)"), which cuts the Brussels-Capital Region from east to west, up to high points in the [Sonian Forest](/wiki/Sonian_Forest "Sonian Forest"), on its southeastern side. In addition to the Senne, tributary streams such as the [Maelbeek](/wiki/Maalbeek "Maalbeek") and the [Woluwe](/wiki/Woluwe "Woluwe"), to the east of the region, account for significant elevation differences. [Brussels' central boulevards](/wiki/Central_Boulevards_of_Brussels "Central Boulevards of Brussels") are 15 m (49 ft) above sea level.[[107]](#cite_note-111) Contrary to popular belief, the highest point (at 127.5 m (418 ft)) is not near the Place de l'Altitude Cent/Hoogte Honderdplein in [Forest](/wiki/Forest,_Belgium "Forest, Belgium"), but at the Drève des Deux Montages/Tweebergendreef in the Sonian Forest.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate")]

Brussels experiences an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*) with warm summers and cool winters.[[109]](#cite_note-Brussels,+Brussels+Capital,+Belgium&units=|title=Brussels,_Belgium_Climate_Classification_Köppen_(Weatherbase)-113) Proximity to coastal areas influences the area's climate by sending marine air masses from the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). Nearby wetlands also ensure a maritime temperate climate. On average (based on measurements in the period 1981–2010), there are approximately 135 days of rain per year in the Brussels-Capital Region. Snowfall is infrequent, averaging 17 days per year. The city also often experiences violent thunderstorms in the summer months.

## As a capital

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: As a capital")]

Despite its name, the Brussels-Capital Region is not the capital of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). Article 194 of the [Belgian Constitution](/wiki/Belgian_Constitution "Belgian Constitution") establishes that the capital of Belgium is the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"), the municipality in the region that is the city's core.[[14]](#cite_note-article_194-16)

The City of Brussels is home to many national institutions. The [Royal Palace of Brussels](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Brussels "Royal Palace of Brussels"), where the [King of the Belgians](/wiki/King_of_the_Belgians "King of the Belgians") exercises his prerogatives as head of state,[[114]](#cite_note-118) is situated alongside [Brussels Park](/wiki/Brussels_Park "Brussels Park") (not to be confused with the [Royal Palace of Laeken](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Laeken "Royal Palace of Laeken"), the official home of the [Belgian royal family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium")).[[115]](#cite_note-119) The [Palace of the Nation](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Nation "Palace of the Nation") is located on the opposite side of this park, and is the seat of the [Belgian Federal Parliament](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Parliament "Belgian Federal Parliament").[[116]](#cite_note-120) The office of the [Prime Minister of Belgium](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Belgium "Prime Minister of Belgium"), colloquially called *Law Street 16* (French: *16, rue de la Loi*, Dutch: *Wetstraat 16*), is located adjacent to this building. It is also where the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_(Belgium) "Council of Ministers (Belgium)") holds its meetings. The [Court of Cassation](/wiki/Court_of_Cassation_(Belgium) "Court of Cassation (Belgium)"), Belgium's main court, has its seat in the [Palace of Justice](/wiki/Palace_of_Justice,_Brussels "Palace of Justice, Brussels"). Other important institutions in the City of Brussels are the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Belgium "Constitutional Court of Belgium"), the [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Belgium) "Council of State (Belgium)"), the [Court of Audit](/wiki/Court_of_Audit_of_Belgium "Court of Audit of Belgium"), the [Royal Belgian Mint](/wiki/Royal_Belgian_Mint "Royal Belgian Mint") and the [National Bank of Belgium](/wiki/National_Bank_of_Belgium "National Bank of Belgium").

The City of Brussels is also the capital of both the [French Community of Belgium](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium")[[15]](#cite_note-décret-17) and the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community").[[17]](#cite_note-decreet-19) The [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government") have their seats in Brussels,[[117]](#cite_note-121) and so do the [Parliament of the French Community](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_French_Community "Parliament of the French Community") and the [Government of the French Community](/wiki/Government_of_the_French_Community "Government of the French Community").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Brussel_Koninklijk_paleis.jpg/960px-Brussel_Koninklijk_paleis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussel_Koninklijk_paleis.jpg)

The [Royal Palace of Brussels](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Brussels "Royal Palace of Brussels"), the official palace of the [King and Queen of the Belgians](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium")

The 19 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Belgium "Municipalities of Belgium") (French: *communes*, Dutch: *gemeenten*) of the Brussels-Capital [Region](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium#Regions "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") are political subdivisions with individual responsibilities for the handling of local level duties, such as law enforcement and the upkeep of schools and roads within its borders.[[118]](#cite_note-list-122)[[119]](#cite_note-OECD-123) Municipal administration is also conducted by a mayor, a council, and an executive.[[119]](#cite_note-OECD-123)

In 1831, Belgium was divided into 2,739 municipalities, including the 19 currently located in the Brussels-Capital Region.[[120]](#cite_note-roots-124) Unlike most of the municipalities in Belgium, the ones now located in the Brussels-Capital Region were not merged with others during mergers occurring in 1964, 1970, and 1975.[[120]](#cite_note-roots-124) However, a few neighbouring municipalities have been merged into the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"), including [Laeken](/wiki/Laeken "Laeken"), [Haren](/wiki/Haren,_Belgium "Haren, Belgium") and [Neder-Over-Heembeek](/wiki/Neder-Over-Heembeek "Neder-Over-Heembeek") in 1921.[[121]](#cite_note-Brussels-125) These comprise the northern bulge in the municipality. To the south-east is a strip of land along the [Avenue Louise/Louizalaan](/wiki/Avenue_Louise/Louizalaan "Avenue Louise/Louizalaan") that, in addition to the [Bois de la Cambre/Ter Kamerenbos](/wiki/Bois_de_la_Cambre/Ter_Kamerenbos "Bois de la Cambre/Ter Kamerenbos"), was annexed from [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles") in 1864. Part of the [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles") (ULB)'s [Solbosch/Solbos](/wiki/Neighbourhoods_in_Brussels#Solbosch/Solbos "Neighbourhoods in Brussels") campus is also part of the City of Brussels, partially accounting for the bulge in the south-eastern end.

The largest municipality in area and population is the City of Brussels, covering 33.09 km2 (12.8 sq mi) and with 198,314 inhabitants;[[122]](#cite_note-126) the least populous is [Koekelberg](/wiki/Koekelberg "Koekelberg") with 22,648 inhabitants.[[123]](#cite_note-127) The smallest in area is [Saint-Josse-ten-Noode](/wiki/Saint-Josse-ten-Noode "Saint-Josse-ten-Noode"), which is only 1.16 km2 (0.4 sq mi), but still has the highest population density in the region, with 23,486/km2 (60,830/sq mi).[[124]](#cite_note-128) [Watermael-Boitsfort](/wiki/Watermael-Boitsfort "Watermael-Boitsfort") has the lowest population density in the region, with 1,953/km2 (5,060/sq mi).[[125]](#cite_note-129)

There is much controversy on the division of 19 municipalities for a highly urbanised region, which is considered as (half of) one city by most people. Some politicians mock the "19 baronies" and want to merge the municipalities under one city council and one mayor.[[126]](#cite_note-130)[[127]](#cite_note-131) That would lower the number of politicians needed to govern Brussels, and centralise the power over the city to make decisions easier, thus reduce the overall running costs. The current municipalities could be transformed into districts with limited responsibilities, similar to the current structure of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") or to structures of other capitals like the [boroughs](/wiki/London_boroughs "London boroughs") in [London](/wiki/London "London") or *[arrondissements](/wiki/Arrondissements_of_Paris "Arrondissements of Paris")* in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), to keep politics close enough to the citizen.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

In the 2010s, [Molenbeek-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Molenbeek-Saint-Jean "Molenbeek-Saint-Jean") gained international attention as the [base](/wiki/Brussels_ISIL_terror_cell "Brussels ISIL terror cell") for [Islamist terrorists](/wiki/Islamist_terrorists "Islamist terrorists") who carried out attacks in both [Paris](/wiki/November_2015_Paris_attacks "November 2015 Paris attacks") and [Brussels](/wiki/2016_Brussels_bombings "2016 Brussels bombings").[[129]](#cite_note-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134)[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136)[[133]](#cite_note-137)

## Brussels-Capital Region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Brussels-Capital Region")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Regions_of_Belgium.svg/250px-Regions_of_Belgium.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Regions_of_Belgium.svg)

Regions of Belgium:

[Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region")

Brussels-Capital Region

[Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region")

### Political status and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Political status and administration")]

See also: [Partition of Belgium § Brussels](/wiki/Partition_of_Belgium#Brussels "Partition of Belgium"); and [Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium § Brussels](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium#Brussels "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium")

The Brussels-Capital Region is one of the three federated regions of Belgium, alongside the [Walloon Region](/wiki/Walloon_Region "Walloon Region") and the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"). Geographically and linguistically, it is a bilingual [enclave](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave") in the monolingual Flemish Region. Regions are one component of Belgium's institutions; the three communities being the other component. Brussels' inhabitants deal with either the [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") or the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") for matters such as culture and education, as well as a [Common Community](/wiki/Common_Community_Commission "Common Community Commission") for competencies that do not belong exclusively to either Community, such as healthcare and social [welfare](/wiki/Welfare_spending "Welfare spending").

Upon the reorganisation and split of the [Province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant") in 1995, the Brussels Region ceased to belong to any of the [provinces of Belgium](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"), nor was it subdivided into provinces itself. However, the [Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital"), whose borders coincide with the Brussels Region, continued to exist, and the areas of provincial jurisdiction were assumed by the newly created [Governor of the Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Governor_of_the_Administrative_Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Governor of the Administrative Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital") and the Brussels regional institutions and community commissions. In 2014, the office of the Governor was formally abolished and replaced in September 2015 by the office of the "High official of the Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital", who retained responsibility for [crisis management](/wiki/Crisis_management "Crisis management") and [emergency planning](/wiki/Emergency_planning "Emergency planning"), while responsibility for [crime prevention](/wiki/Crime_prevention "Crime prevention") was transferred to the [Minister-President of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Minister-President_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Minister-President of the Brussels-Capital Region").[[134]](#cite_note-High-138)

### Institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Institutions")]

Main articles: [Government of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Government_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Government of the Brussels-Capital Region") and [Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Brussels_Parliament_building_%282%29.jpg/250px-Brussels_Parliament_building_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_Parliament_building_(2).jpg)

The [Brussels Parliament building](/wiki/Brussels_Parliament_building "Brussels Parliament building"), housing the [Brussels Regional Parliament](/wiki/Brussels_Regional_Parliament "Brussels Regional Parliament")

The Brussels-Capital Region is governed by a [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region") of 89 members (72 French-speaking, 17 Dutch-speaking—parties are organised on a linguistic basis) and an eight-member regional cabinet consisting of a [minister-president](/wiki/Minister-President_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Minister-President of the Brussels-Capital Region"), four ministers and three [state secretaries](/wiki/Secretary_of_state "Secretary of state"). By law, the cabinet must comprise two French-speaking and two Dutch-speaking ministers, one Dutch-speaking secretary of state and two French-speaking secretaries of state. The minister-president does not count against the language quota, but in practice every minister-president has been a bilingual francophone. The regional parliament can enact [ordinances](/wiki/Ordinance_(Belgium) "Ordinance (Belgium)") (French: *ordonnances*, Dutch: *ordonnanties*), which have equal status as a national legislative act.

Nineteen of the 72 French-speaking members of the Brussels Parliament are also members of the [Parliament of the French Community of Belgium](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_French_Community_of_Belgium "Parliament of the French Community of Belgium"), and, until 2004, this was also the case for six Dutch-speaking members, who were at the same time members of the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament"). Now, people voting for a Flemish party have to vote separately for six directly elected members of the Flemish Parliament.

### Agglomeration of Brussels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Agglomeration of Brussels")]

Before the creation of the Brussels-Capital Region, regional competences in the 19 municipalities were performed by the Brussels Agglomeration. The Brussels Agglomeration was an administrative division established in 1971. This decentralised administrative public body also assumed jurisdiction over areas that, elsewhere in Belgium, were exercised by municipalities or provinces.[[135]](#cite_note-139)

The Brussels Agglomeration had a separate legislative council, but the by-laws it enacted did not have legislative status. The only election of the council took place on 21 November 1971. The working of the council was subject to many difficulties caused by the linguistic and socio-economic tensions between the two communities.

After the creation of the Brussels-Capital Region, the Brussels Agglomeration was never formally abolished, although it no longer has a purpose.

## French and Flemish communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: French and Flemish communities")]

Main articles: [French Community Commission](/wiki/French_Community_Commission "French Community Commission") and [Flemish Community Commission](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Communities_of_Belgium.svg/250px-Communities_of_Belgium.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Communities_of_Belgium.svg)

Communities of Belgium:

[Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") / Dutch language area

Flemish & French Community / bilingual language area

[French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") / French language area

[German-speaking Community](/wiki/German-speaking_Community "German-speaking Community") / German language area

The [French Community](/wiki/French_Community_of_Belgium "French Community of Belgium") and the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") exercise their powers in Brussels through two community-specific public authorities: the [French Community Commission](/wiki/French_Community_Commission "French Community Commission") (French: *Commission communautaire française* or COCOF) and the [Flemish Community Commission](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission") (Dutch: *Vlaamse Gemeenschapscommissie* or VGC). These two bodies each have an assembly composed of the members from each linguistic group in the [Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "Parliament of the Brussels-Capital Region"). They also have a board composed of the ministers and secretaries of state from each linguistic group in the Government of the Brussels-Capital Region.

The French Community Commission also has another capacity: some of the French Community's legislative powers have been devolved to the Walloon Region (for the French language area of Belgium) and to the French Community Commission (for the bilingual language area).[[136]](#cite_note-140) The Flemish Community, however, did the opposite; it merged the Flemish Region into the Flemish Community.[[137]](#cite_note-141) This is related the two linguistic groups having different conceptions, with one focusing more on the Communities and the other more on the Regions, causing an asymmetrical [federalism](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism"). Because of this devolution, the French Community Commission can enact [decrees](/wiki/Decree "Decree"), which are legislative acts.

### Common Community Commission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Common Community Commission")]

A bi-communitarian public authority, the [Common Community Commission](/wiki/Common_Community_Commission "Common Community Commission") (French: *Commission communautaire commune*, COCOM, Dutch: *Gemeenschappelijke Gemeenschapscommissie*, GGC) also exists. Its assembly is composed of the members of the regional parliament, and its board are the region's ministers—not the secretaries of state—with the minister-president not having the right to vote. This commission has two capacities: it is a decentralised administrative public body, responsible for implementing cultural policies of common interest. It can give subsidies and enact [by-laws](/wiki/By-law "By-law"). Additionally, it can also enact ordinances, which have equal status as a national legislative act, in the field of community welfare: in the Brussels-Capital Region, both the French Community and the Flemish Community can exercise powers in the field of welfare, but only in regard to institutions that are unilingual (for example, a private French-speaking retirement home or the Dutch-speaking hospital of the [Vrije Universiteit Brussel](/wiki/Vrije_Universiteit_Brussel "Vrije Universiteit Brussel")). The Common Community Commission is responsible for policies aiming directly at private persons or at bilingual institutions (for example, the centres for social welfare of the 19 municipalities). Its ordinances have to be enacted with a majority in both linguistic groups. Failing such a majority, a new vote can be held, where a majority of at least one third in each linguistic group is sufficient.

## Brussels and the European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Brussels and the European Union")]

Main article: [Brussels and the European Union](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union "Brussels and the European Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Quartier_europ%C3%A9en_Bruxelles_2011-06_CROP.jpg/250px-Quartier_europ%C3%A9en_Bruxelles_2011-06_CROP.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quartier_europ%C3%A9en_Bruxelles_2011-06_CROP.jpg)

Aerial view of Brussels' [European Quarter](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union#European_Quarter "Brussels and the European Union"), hosting most of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU)'s institutions

Brussels serves as *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* capital of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU), hosting the major political [institutions of the Union](/wiki/Institutions_of_the_European_Union "Institutions of the European Union").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey2007-1) The EU has not declared a capital formally, though the [Treaty of Amsterdam](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amsterdam "Treaty of Amsterdam") formally gives Brussels the seat of the [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission") (the executive branch of government) and the [Council of the European Union](/wiki/Council_of_the_European_Union "Council of the European Union") (a legislative institution made up from executives of member states).[[138]](#cite_note-ENA_COMM_SEAT-142)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[139]](#cite_note-EC_EiB-143)[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] It locates the formal seat of [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") in [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), where votes take place, with the council, on the proposals made by the Commission. However, meetings of political groups and committee groups are formally given to Brussels, along with a set number of plenary sessions. Three quarters of Parliament sessions now take place at its [Brussels hemicycle](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold").[[140]](#cite_note-144) Between 2002 and 2004, the [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") also fixed its seat in the city.[[141]](#cite_note-Dragoman-145) In 2014, the Union hosted a [G7 summit](/wiki/40th_G7_summit "40th G7 summit") in the city.[[99]](#cite_note-:0-103)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Place_du_Luxembourg_889_Wide.jpg/330px-Place_du_Luxembourg_889_Wide.jpg)](/wiki/File:Place_du_Luxembourg_889_Wide.jpg)

The [Place du Luxembourg/Luxemburgplein](/wiki/Place_du_Luxembourg "Place du Luxembourg") with the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") in the background

Brussels, along with [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City") and Strasbourg, began to host European institutions in 1957, soon becoming the centre of activities, as the Commission and Council based their activities in what has become the [European Quarter](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union#European_Quarter "Brussels and the European Union"), in the east of the city.[[138]](#cite_note-ENA_COMM_SEAT-142) Early building in Brussels was sporadic and uncontrolled, with little planning. The current major buildings are the [Berlaymont building](/wiki/Berlaymont_building "Berlaymont building") of the Commission, symbolic of the quarter as a whole, the [Europa building](/wiki/Europa_building "Europa building") of the Council and the [Espace Léopold](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold") of the Parliament.[[139]](#cite_note-EC_EiB-143) In recent years, the presence has increased considerably, with the Commission alone occupying 865,000 m2 (9,310,000 sq ft) within the European Quarter (a quarter of the total office space in Brussels).[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDemey2007-1) The concentration and density has caused concern that the presence of the institutions has created a *[ghetto](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto") effect* in that part of the city.[[142]](#cite_note-EUO_buildings-146) However, the European presence has contributed significantly to the importance of Brussels as an international centre.[[143]](#cite_note-E!Sarp-147)

## International institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: International institutions")]

Brussels has, since [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), become the administrative centre of many international organisations. The city is the political and administrative centre of the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Treaty_Organization "North Atlantic Treaty Organization") (NATO). [NATO's Brussels headquarters](/wiki/NATO_headquarters "NATO headquarters") houses [29 embassies](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_to_NATO "List of diplomatic missions to NATO") and brings together over 4,500 staff from allied nations, their militaries, and civil service personnel. Many other international organisations such as the [World Customs Organization](/wiki/World_Customs_Organization "World Customs Organization") and [Eurocontrol](/wiki/Eurocontrol "Eurocontrol"), as well as international corporations, have their main institutions in the city. In addition, the main international [trade union](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union") confederations have their headquarters there: the [European Trade Union Confederation](/wiki/European_Trade_Union_Confederation "European Trade Union Confederation") (ETUC), the [International Confederation of Free Trade Unions](/wiki/International_Confederation_of_Free_Trade_Unions "International Confederation of Free Trade Unions") (ICFTU) and the [World Confederation of Labour](/wiki/World_Confederation_of_Labour "World Confederation of Labour") (WCL).

Brussels is third in the number of international conferences it hosts,[[144]](#cite_note-ULB_Int-148) also becoming one of the largest convention centres in the world.[[145]](#cite_note-diplomatie-149) The presence of the EU and the other international bodies has, for example, led to there being more ambassadors and journalists in Brussels than in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[143]](#cite_note-E!Sarp-147) The city hosts 120 international institutions, 181 [embassies](/wiki/Embassies "Embassies") (*intra muros*) and more than 2,500 [diplomats](/wiki/Diplomacy "Diplomacy"), making it the second centre of diplomatic relations in the world (after [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")). [International schools](/wiki/International_school "International school") have also been established to serve this presence.[[145]](#cite_note-diplomatie-149) The "international community" in Brussels numbers at least 70,000 people.[[146]](#cite_note-150) In 2009, there were an estimated 286 [lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying") consultancies known to work in Brussels.[[147]](#cite_note-151) Finally, Brussels has more than 1,400 [NGOs](/wiki/NGO "NGO").[[148]](#cite_note-152)[[149]](#cite_note-153)

### North Atlantic Treaty Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: North Atlantic Treaty Organization")]

Main article: [NATO headquarters](/wiki/NATO_headquarters "NATO headquarters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/2018_NATO_Summit_in_Brussels_%2829510554308%29.jpg/250px-2018_NATO_Summit_in_Brussels_%2829510554308%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_NATO_Summit_in_Brussels_(29510554308).jpg)

Flags of [NATO member states](/wiki/Member_states_of_NATO "Member states of NATO") wave at the entrance of [NATO's headquarters](/wiki/NATO_headquarters "NATO headquarters") in [Haren](/wiki/Haren,_Belgium "Haren, Belgium")

The [Treaty of Brussels](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels "Treaty of Brussels"), which was signed on 17 March 1948 between Belgium, [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), was a prelude to the establishment of the [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization") [military alliance](/wiki/Military_alliance "Military alliance") that later became the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Treaty_Organization "North Atlantic Treaty Organization") (NATO).[[150]](#cite_note-154) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit), the alliance consists of 32 independent member countries across [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and Europe. Several countries also have diplomatic missions to NATO [through embassies in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_to_NATO "List of diplomatic missions to NATO"). Since 1949, a number of [NATO Summits](/wiki/NATO_summit "NATO summit") have been held in Brussels,[[151]](#cite_note-155) the most recent taking place in June 2021.[[102]](#cite_note-:9-106) The organisation's political and administrative [headquarters](/wiki/NATO_headquarters "NATO headquarters") are located on the Boulevard Léopold III/Leopold III-laan in [Haren](/wiki/Haren,_Belgium "Haren, Belgium"), on the north-eastern perimeter of the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels").[[152]](#cite_note-156) A new €750 million headquarters building begun in 2010 and was completed in 2017.[[153]](#cite_note-157)

### Eurocontrol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Eurocontrol")]

Main article: [Eurocontrol](/wiki/Eurocontrol "Eurocontrol")

The European Organisation for the Safety of Air Navigation, commonly known as Eurocontrol, is an [international organisation](/wiki/International_organisation "International organisation") that coordinates and plans [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") across European [airspace](/wiki/Airspace "Airspace"). The corporation was founded in 1960 and has 41 member states.[[154]](#cite_note-History_Book-158) Its headquarters are located in Haren, Brussels.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Brussels](/wiki/Demographics_of_Brussels "Demographics of Brussels")

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/EU_NUTS_2_population_density_2007.svg/250px-EU_NUTS_2_population_density_2007.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_NUTS_2_population_density_2007.svg)

[Population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") of Europe. Brussels is located between the largest urban centres.

Brussels is located in one of the most [urbanised regions of Europe](/wiki/Blue_Banana "Blue Banana"), between [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), the [Rhine-Ruhr](/wiki/Rhine-Ruhr "Rhine-Ruhr") (Germany), and the [Randstad](/wiki/Randstad "Randstad") (Netherlands). The Brussels-Capital Region has a population of around 1.2 million and has witnessed, in recent years, a remarkable increase in its population. In general, the population of Brussels is younger than the national average, and the gap between rich and poor is wider.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

Brussels is the core of a built-up area that extends well beyond the region's limits. Sometimes referred to as the urban area of Brussels (French: *aire urbaine de Bruxelles*, Dutch: *stedelijk gebied van Brussel*) or Greater Brussels (French: *Grand-Bruxelles*, Dutch: *Groot-Brussel*), this area extends over a large part of the two Brabant provinces, including much of the surrounding [arrondissement of Halle-Vilvoorde](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Halle-Vilvoorde "Arrondissement of Halle-Vilvoorde") and some small parts of the [arrondissement of Leuven](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Leuven "Arrondissement of Leuven") in [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), as well as the northern part of [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant").

The metropolitan area of Brussels is divided into three levels. Firstly, the central agglomeration (French: *agglomération opérationnelle*, Dutch: *geoperationaliseerde agglomeratie*) within the regional borders, with a population of 1,218,255 inhabitants.[[27]](#cite_note-:8-30) Adding the closest suburbs (French: *banlieues*, Dutch: *buitenwijken*) gives a total population of 1,831,496. Including the outer [commuter](/wiki/Commuter "Commuter") area (French: *zone résidentielle des migrants alternants*, Dutch: *forensenwoonzone*), the population is 2,676,701.[[29]](#cite_note-BelMetr-32)[[30]](#cite_note-:5-33) Brussels is also part of a wider [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") extending towards the cities of [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), known as the [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond"), as well as the province of [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant"), in total home to over 5 million people (a little more than 40% of the Belgium's total population).[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_MeeterenBoussauwDerudderWitlox2016-34)[[156]](#cite_note-Meijers-2007-160)

There have been numerous migrations towards Brussels since the end of the 18th century, when the city acted as a common destination for [political refugees](/wiki/Political_refugees "Political refugees") from neighbouring or more distant countries, particularly France.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumont1996307–8,_312,_319-163) From 1871, many of the [Paris Communards](/wiki/Communards "Communards") fled to Brussels, where they received political asylum. Other notable international exiles living in Brussels at the time included [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo"), [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), [Pierre-Joseph Proudhon](/wiki/Pierre-Joseph_Proudhon "Pierre-Joseph Proudhon"), [Georges Boulanger](/wiki/Georges_Boulanger "Georges Boulanger"), [Paul Verlaine](/wiki/Paul_Verlaine "Paul Verlaine"), [Arthur Rimbaud](/wiki/Arthur_Rimbaud "Arthur Rimbaud"), and [Léon Daudet](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Daudet "Léon Daudet"), to name a few.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDumont1996307–8,_312,_319-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164) Attracted by the industrial opportunities, many workers moved in, first from the other [Belgian provinces](/wiki/Belgian_provinces "Belgian provinces") (mainly rural residents from [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"))[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuron201680–82-165) and France, then from [Southern European](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), and more recently from [Eastern European](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [African](/wiki/Africa "Africa") countries.

Since the second half of the 20th century, Brussels has been home to a large number of immigrants and [émigré](/wiki/%C3%89migr%C3%A9 "Émigré") communities, as well as labour migrants, former foreign students or [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate"), and many Belgian families in Brussels can claim at least one foreign grandparent. At the last Belgian census in 1991, 63.7% of inhabitants in Brussels-Capital Region answered that they were Belgian citizens, born as such in Belgium, indicating that more than a third of residents had not been born in the country.[[162]](#cite_note-philippevanparijs-166)[[163]](#cite_note-ibsa-167) According to [Statbel](/wiki/Statbel "Statbel") (the Belgian Statistical Office), in 2020, taking into account the parents' nationality of birth, 74.3% of the population of the Brussels-Capital Region was of foreign origin and 41.8% was of non-European origin (including 28.7% of African origin). Among those aged under 18, 88% were of foreign origin and 57% of non-European origin (including 42.4% of African origin).[[164]](#cite_note-micheletribalat.fr-168)

This large concentration of immigrants and their descendants includes many of [Moroccan](/wiki/Moroccans_in_Belgium "Moroccans in Belgium") (mainly [Riffian](/wiki/Riffian_people "Riffian people") and other [Berbers](/wiki/Berbers "Berbers")) and [Turkish](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") ancestry, together with French-speaking black Africans from former [Belgian colonies](/wiki/Belgian_overseas_colonies "Belgian overseas colonies"), such as the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"), [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda") and [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi"). Many immigrants were [naturalised](/wiki/Naturalised "Naturalised") following the great 1991 reform of the naturalisation process. In 2012, about 32% of city residents were of non-Belgian [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") origin (mainly expatriates from France, Romania, Italy, Spain, Poland, and Portugal) and 36% were of another background, mostly from Morocco, Turkey and [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"). Among all major migrant groups from outside the EU, a majority of the permanent residents have acquired Belgian nationality.[[165]](#cite_note-169)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Languages")]

See also: [Francization of Brussels](/wiki/Francization_of_Brussels "Francization of Brussels")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Languages_spoken_at_home_in_the_Brussels_Capital_Region_%282013%29.PNG/250px-Languages_spoken_at_home_in_the_Brussels_Capital_Region_%282013%29.PNG)](/wiki/File:Languages_spoken_at_home_in_the_Brussels_Capital_Region_(2013).PNG)
