Brussels was historically [Dutch-speaking](/wiki/Dutch-speaking "Dutch-speaking"), using the [Brabantian dialect](/wiki/Brabantian_dialect "Brabantian dialect"),[[167]](#cite_note-tokyo-171)[[168]](#cite_note-jaumain-172)[[169]](#cite_note-roegiest-173) but since the 19th century[[167]](#cite_note-tokyo-171)[[170]](#cite_note-marynissen-174) [French](/wiki/Belgian_French "Belgian French") has become the city's predominant language.[[171]](#cite_note-rudi3-175) The main cause of this transition was the rapid [assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") of the local [Flemish population](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people"),[[172]](#cite_note-kramer-176)[[167]](#cite_note-tokyo-171)[[173]](#cite_note-baetens-177)[[174]](#cite_note-ernst-178)[[169]](#cite_note-roegiest-173) amplified by immigration from [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia").[[167]](#cite_note-tokyo-171)[[175]](#cite_note-vermeersch-179) The [rise of French](/wiki/Francization "Francization") in public life gradually began by the end of the 18th century,[[176]](#cite_note-poirier-180)[[177]](#cite_note-rousseaux-181) quickly accelerating after [Belgian independence](/wiki/Belgian_independence "Belgian independence").[[178]](#cite_note-degroof-182)[[179]](#cite_note-gubin-183) Dutch—of which [standardisation](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") in Belgium was still very weak[[180]](#cite_note-balans-184)[[181]](#cite_note-busekist-185)[[179]](#cite_note-gubin-183)—could not compete with French, which was the exclusive language of the judiciary, the administration, the army, education, cultural life and the media, and thus necessary for [social mobility](/wiki/Social_mobility "Social mobility").[[182]](#cite_note-bitsch-186)[[183]](#cite_note-tétart-187)[[168]](#cite_note-jaumain-172)[[184]](#cite_note-kok-188)[[170]](#cite_note-marynissen-174) The value and prestige of the French language were universally acknowledged[[168]](#cite_note-jaumain-172)[[185]](#cite_note-bogaert-189)[[172]](#cite_note-kramer-176)[[179]](#cite_note-gubin-183)[[186]](#cite_note-hasquin2-190)[[187]](#cite_note-vrints-191) to such an extent that after 1880,[[188]](#cite_note-veron-192)[[189]](#cite_note-velthoven-193)[[180]](#cite_note-balans-184) and more particularly after the turn of the 20th century,[[179]](#cite_note-gubin-183) proficiency in French among Dutch-speakers in Brussels increased spectacularly.[[190]](#cite_note-blampain-194)

Although a majority of the population remained bilingual until the second half of the 20th century,[[190]](#cite_note-blampain-194)[[172]](#cite_note-kramer-176) family transmission of the historic Brabantian dialect[[191]](#cite_note-witte-195) declined,[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller1994-196) leading to an increase of monolingual French-speakers from 1910 onwards.[[185]](#cite_note-bogaert-189)[[193]](#cite_note-metsenaere-thuis-197) From the mid-20th century, the number of monolingual French-speakers surpassed the number of mostly bilingual Flemish inhabitants.[[194]](#cite_note-brio4-198) This process of assimilation weakened after the 1960s,[[190]](#cite_note-blampain-194)[[195]](#cite_note-mares-199) as the [language border](/wiki/Language_border "Language border") was fixed, the status of Dutch as an official language of Belgium was reinforced,[[196]](#cite_note-depré-200) and the economic centre of gravity shifted northward to [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders").[[180]](#cite_note-balans-184)[[188]](#cite_note-veron-192) However, with the continuing arrival of immigrants and the post-war emergence of Brussels as a [centre of international politics](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union "Brussels and the European Union"), the relative position of Dutch continued to decline.[[197]](#cite_note-brusselsstudies-201)[[170]](#cite_note-marynissen-174)[[198]](#cite_note-janssens-202)[[199]](#cite_note-detant-203)[[190]](#cite_note-blampain-194)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller1994-196) Furthermore, as Brussels' urban area expanded,[[200]](#cite_note-meynen-204) a further number of Dutch-speaking municipalities in the [Brussels periphery](/wiki/Brussels_Periphery "Brussels Periphery") also became predominantly French-speaking.[[196]](#cite_note-depré-200)[[201]](#cite_note-klinkenberg-205) This phenomenon of expanding Francisation—dubbed "oil slick" by its opponents[[172]](#cite_note-kramer-176)[[202]](#cite_note-kesteloot-206)[[190]](#cite_note-blampain-194)—is, together with the future of Brussels,[[203]](#cite_note-frognier-207) one of the most controversial topics in [Belgian politics](/wiki/Belgian_politics "Belgian politics").[[188]](#cite_note-veron-192)[[183]](#cite_note-tétart-187)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Brussels_signs.jpg/250px-Brussels_signs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_signs.jpg)

[Bilingual](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism") French and Dutch street signs in Brussels

Since its creation in 1989, the Brussels-Capital Region has been legally bilingual, with both French and Dutch having official status,[[204]](#cite_note-constitution-208) as is the administration of the 19 municipalities.[[197]](#cite_note-brusselsstudies-201) The creation of this bilingual, full-fledged region, with its own competencies and jurisdiction, had long been hampered by different visions of Belgian federalism. Nevertheless, some communitarian issues remain.[[205]](#cite_note-jacobs-209)[[206]](#cite_note-debruycker-210) Flemish political parties demanded, for decades, that the Flemish part of [Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde](/wiki/Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde "Brussels-Halle-Vilvoorde") (BHV) *arrondissement* be separated from the Brussels Region (which made Halle-Vilvoorde a monolingual Flemish electoral and judicial district). BHV was divided mid-2012. The French-speaking population regards the language border as artificial[[207]](#cite_note-211) and demands the extension of the bilingual region to at least all six [municipalities with language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities") in the surroundings of Brussels.[[e]](#cite_note-212) Flemish politicians have strongly rejected these proposals.[[208]](#cite_note-question-213)[[209]](#cite_note-214)[[210]](#cite_note-215)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Arrondissement_Brussels-Periphery_Belgium_Map.PNG/250px-Arrondissement_Brussels-Periphery_Belgium_Map.PNG)](/wiki/File:Arrondissement_Brussels-Periphery_Belgium_Map.PNG)

The [municipalities with language facilities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities") (in red) near Brussels

Owing to migration and to its international role, Brussels is home to a large number of native speakers of languages other than French or Dutch. Currently, about half of the population speaks a home language other than these two.[[211]](#cite_note-standaard-216) In 2013, academic research showed that approximately 17% of families spoke none of the official languages in the home, while in a further 23% a foreign language was used alongside French. The share of unilingual French-speaking families had fallen to 38% and that of Dutch-speaking families to 5%, while the percentage of bilingual Dutch-French families reached 17%. At the same time, French remains widely spoken: in 2013, French was spoken "well to perfectly" by 88% of the population, while for Dutch this percentage was only 23% (down from 33% in 2000);[[197]](#cite_note-brusselsstudies-201) the other most commonly known languages were English (30%), Arabic (18%), Spanish (9%), German (7%) and Italian and Turkish (5% each).[[166]](#cite_note-brio2013-170) Meanwhile, surveys from 2023 to 2024 suggest that 29% of the population speaks only languages other than French and Dutch in the home, while residents speak a total of 104 languages, up from 72 in 2001.[[212]](#cite_note-u275-217) Despite the rise of English as a second language in Brussels, including as an unofficial compromise language between French and Dutch, as well as the working language for some of its international businesses and institutions, French remains the *lingua franca* and all public services are conducted exclusively in French or Dutch.[[197]](#cite_note-brusselsstudies-201)

The original dialect of Brussels (known as [Brusselian](/wiki/Brusselian_dialect "Brusselian dialect"), and also sometimes referred to as [Marollian](/wiki/Marollian_dialect "Marollian dialect")),[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller199425-54) a form of [Brabantic](/wiki/Brabantic "Brabantic") (the variant of Dutch spoken in the ancient [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant")) with a significant number of loanwords from French, still survives among a small minority of inhabitants called *Brusseleers*[[52]](#cite_note-evans-55) (or *Brusseleirs*), many of them quite bi- and multilingual, or educated in French and not writing in Dutch.[[213]](#cite_note-winkler-218)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller199425-54) The ethnic and national self-identification of Brussels' inhabitants is nonetheless sometimes quite distinct from the French and Dutch-speaking communities. For the French-speakers, it can vary from Francophone Belgian, *Bruxellois*[[54]](#cite_note-Lerond,_1980_pp._477-57) (French [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") for an inhabitant of Brussels), [Walloon](/wiki/Walloons "Walloons") (for people who migrated from the Walloon Region at an adult age); for Flemings living in Brussels, it is mainly either Dutch-speaking Belgian, Flemish or *Brusselaar* (Dutch demonym for an inhabitant), and often both. For the *Brusseleers*, many simply consider themselves as belonging to Brussels.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETreffers-Daller199425-54)

### Religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Religions")]

Further information: [Religion in Belgium](/wiki/Religion_in_Belgium "Religion in Belgium")

Religions in the Brussels-Capital Region (2016)[[214]](#cite_note-2016religion-219)

1. [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (40.0%)
2. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (23.0%)
3. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (3.00%)
4. Other religions (4.00%)
5. Non-religious (30.0%)

Historically, Brussels has been predominantly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), especially since the expulsion of [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") in the 16th century. This is clear from the large number of historical churches in the region, particularly in the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"). The pre-eminent Catholic [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") in Brussels is the [Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula "Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula"), serving as the [co-cathedral](/wiki/Co-cathedral "Co-cathedral") of the [Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Mechelen%E2%80%93Brussels "Archdiocese of Mechelen–Brussels"). On the north-western side of the region, the [National Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_Brussels "Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Brussels") is a [Minor Basilica](/wiki/Minor_Basilica "Minor Basilica") and [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church"), as well as one of the [largest churches by area in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_church_buildings "List of largest church buildings").[[215]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStéphany200663-220) The [Church of Our Lady of Laeken](/wiki/Church_of_Our_Lady_of_Laeken "Church of Our Lady of Laeken") holds the tombs of many members of the [Belgian royal family](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Belgium "Monarchy of Belgium"), including all the former [Belgian monarchs](/wiki/Belgian_monarchs "Belgian monarchs"), within the [Royal Crypt](/wiki/Royal_Crypt_(Belgium) "Royal Crypt (Belgium)").[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEState2004218-221)[[217]](#cite_note-222)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_%285%29.jpg/250px-Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruxelles_-_Basilique_Nationale_du_Sacr%C3%A9-C%C5%93ur_%C3%A0_Koekelberg_(5).jpg)

The [National Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_Brussels "Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Brussels") in [Koekelberg](/wiki/Koekelberg "Koekelberg")

In reflection of its multicultural makeup, Brussels hosts a variety of religious communities, as well as large numbers of [atheists](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists") and [agnostics](/wiki/Agnostics "Agnostics"). Minority faiths include Islam, [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and Buddhism. According to a 2016 survey, approximately 40% of residents of Brussels declared themselves Catholics (12% were practising Catholics and 28% were non-practising Catholics), 30% were [non-religious](/wiki/Non-religious "Non-religious"), 23% were [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") (19% practising, 4% non-practising), 3% were Protestants and 4% were of another religion.[[214]](#cite_note-2016religion-219)

As guaranteed by Belgian law, recognised religions and non-religious philosophical organisations (French: *organisations laïques*, Dutch: *vrijzinnige levensbeschouwelijke organisaties*)[[218]](#cite_note-berkley-223) enjoy public funding and school courses. It was once the case that every pupil in an official school from 6 to 18 years old had to choose two hours per week of compulsory religious—or non-religious-inspired morals—courses. However, in 2015, the [Belgian Constitutional Court](/wiki/Belgian_Constitutional_Court "Belgian Constitutional Court") ruled religious studies could no longer be required in the [primary](/wiki/Primary_school "Primary school") and [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") educational systems.[[219]](#cite_note-224)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg/250px-Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Mosque_of_Brussels.jpg)

The [Great Mosque of Brussels](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Brussels "Great Mosque of Brussels"), former seat of the Islamic and Cultural Centre of Belgium

Brussels has a large concentration of [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Belgium "Islam in Belgium"), mostly of Moroccan, Turkish, Syrian and Guinean ancestry. The [Great Mosque of Brussels](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Brussels "Great Mosque of Brussels"), located in the [Parc du Cinquantenaire/Jubelpark](/wiki/Cinquantenaire "Cinquantenaire"), is the oldest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") in Brussels and the former seat of the Islamic and Cultural Centre of Belgium.[[220]](#cite_note-225) Belgium does not collect statistics by ethnic background or religious beliefs, so exact figures are unknown.[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETorrekens2007-226) It was estimated that, in 2005, people of Muslim background living in the Brussels Region numbered 256,220 and accounted for 25.5% of the city's population, a much higher concentration than those of the other regions of Belgium.[[222]](#cite_note-BuG_100-227)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

| **Brussels-Capital Region** | **1,180,531** | **212,495** | **18%** |

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Architecture")]

The architecture in Brussels is diverse, and spans from the clashing combination of [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture"), and [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV_style "Louis XIV style") styles on the [Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place") to the [postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern_architecture "Postmodern architecture") buildings of the [EU institutions](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union "Brussels and the European Union").[[223]](#cite_note-228)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Bruxelles_Manneken_Pis.jpg/250px-Bruxelles_Manneken_Pis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bruxelles_Manneken_Pis.jpg)

*[Manneken Pis](/wiki/Manneken_Pis "Manneken Pis")*, a well-known public sculpture

Very little [medieval architecture](/wiki/Medieval_architecture "Medieval architecture") is preserved in Brussels. Buildings from that period are mostly found in the historical centre (called the Îlot Sacré), [Saint Géry/Sint-Goriks](/wiki/Saint-G%C3%A9ry_Island "Saint-Géry Island") and Sainte-Catherine/Sint Katelijne neighbourhoods. The [Brabantine Gothic](/wiki/Brabantine_Gothic "Brabantine Gothic") [Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula "Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula") remains a prominent feature in the skyline of downtown Brussels. Isolated portions of the [first city walls](/wiki/First_walls_of_Brussels "First walls of Brussels") were saved from destruction and can be seen to this day. One of the only remains of the [second walls](/wiki/Second_walls_of_Brussels "Second walls of Brussels") is the [Halle Gate](/wiki/Halle_Gate "Halle Gate"). The Grand-Place is the main attraction in the city centre and has been a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site") since 1998.[[224]](#cite_note-229) The square is dominated by the 15th century [Flamboyant](/wiki/Flamboyant "Flamboyant") [Town Hall](/wiki/Brussels_Town_Hall "Brussels Town Hall"), the [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") *[Breadhouse](/wiki/Museum_of_the_City_of_Brussels "Museum of the City of Brussels")* and the Baroque [guildhalls](/wiki/Guildhall "Guildhall") of the former [Guilds of Brussels](/wiki/Guilds_of_Brussels "Guilds of Brussels"). *[Manneken Pis](/wiki/Manneken_Pis "Manneken Pis")*, a fountain containing a small [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") sculpture of a urinating youth, is a tourist attraction and [symbol](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol") of the city.[[225]](#cite_note-230)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Parterre_de_fleurs_Grand-place_de_Bruxelles_2016.jpg/960px-Parterre_de_fleurs_Grand-place_de_Bruxelles_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parterre_de_fleurs_Grand-place_de_Bruxelles_2016.jpg)

The [Grand-Place of Brussels](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place"), a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

The [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") style of the 18th and 19th centuries is represented in the [Royal Quarter](/wiki/Royal_Quarter "Royal Quarter")/[Coudenberg](/wiki/Palace_of_Coudenberg "Palace of Coudenberg") area, around [Brussels Park](/wiki/Brussels_Park "Brussels Park") and the [Place Royale/Koningsplein](/wiki/Place_Royale,_Brussels "Place Royale, Brussels"). Examples include the [Royal Palace](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Brussels "Royal Palace of Brussels"), the [Church of St. James on Coudenberg](/wiki/Church_of_St._James_on_Coudenberg "Church of St. James on Coudenberg"), the [Palace of the Nation](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Nation "Palace of the Nation") (Parliament building), the [Academy Palace](/wiki/Academy_Palace "Academy Palace"), the [Palace of Charles of Lorraine](/wiki/Palace_of_Charles_of_Lorraine "Palace of Charles of Lorraine"), the [Palace of the Count of Flanders](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Count_of_Flanders "Palace of the Count of Flanders") and the [Egmont Palace](/wiki/Egmont_Palace "Egmont Palace"). Other uniform neoclassical ensembles can be found around the [Place des Martyrs/Martelaarsplein](/wiki/Place_des_Martyrs,_Brussels "Place des Martyrs, Brussels") and the [Place des Barricades/Barricadenplein](/wiki/Place_des_Barricades "Place des Barricades"). Some additional landmarks in the centre are the [Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries](/wiki/Royal_Saint-Hubert_Galleries "Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries") (1847), one of the oldest covered shopping arcades in Europe, the [Congress Column](/wiki/Congress_Column "Congress Column") (1859), the [Bourse Palace](/wiki/Bourse_Palace "Bourse Palace") (1873) and the [Palace of Justice](/wiki/Palace_of_Justice,_Brussels "Palace of Justice, Brussels") (1883). The latter, designed by [Joseph Poelaert](/wiki/Joseph_Poelaert "Joseph Poelaert"), in [eclectic](/wiki/Eclecticism_in_architecture "Eclecticism in architecture") style, is reputed to be the largest building constructed in the 19th century.[[226]](#cite_note-231)

Located outside the historical centre, in a greener environment bordering the [European Quarter](/wiki/Brussels_and_the_European_Union#European_Quarter "Brussels and the European Union"), are the [Parc du Cinquantenaire/Jubelpark](/wiki/Cinquantenaire "Cinquantenaire") with its [memorial arcade](/wiki/Arcade_du_Cinquantenaire "Arcade du Cinquantenaire") and nearby museums, and in [Laeken](/wiki/Laeken "Laeken"), the [Royal Palace of Laeken](/wiki/Royal_Palace_of_Laeken "Royal Palace of Laeken") and the [Royal Domain](/wiki/Royal_Domain_of_Laeken "Royal Domain of Laeken") with its large [greenhouses](/wiki/Royal_Greenhouses_of_Laeken "Royal Greenhouses of Laeken"), as well as the [Museums of the Far East](/wiki/Museums_of_the_Far_East "Museums of the Far East").

Also particularly striking are the buildings in the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau_in_Brussels "Art Nouveau in Brussels") style, most famously by the Belgian architects [Victor Horta](/wiki/Victor_Horta "Victor Horta"), [Paul Hankar](/wiki/Paul_Hankar "Paul Hankar") and [Henry Van de Velde](/wiki/Henry_Van_de_Velde "Henry Van de Velde").[[227]](#cite_note-232)[[228]](#cite_note-233) Some of Brussels' municipalities, such as [Schaerbeek](/wiki/Schaerbeek "Schaerbeek"), [Etterbeek](/wiki/Etterbeek "Etterbeek"), [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles"), and [Saint-Gilles](/wiki/Saint-Gilles,_Belgium "Saint-Gilles, Belgium"), were developed during the heyday of Art Nouveau and have many buildings in that style. The [Major Town Houses of the Architect Victor Horta](/wiki/Major_Town_Houses_of_the_Architect_Victor_Horta "Major Town Houses of the Architect Victor Horta")—[Hôtel Tassel](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_Tassel "Hôtel Tassel") (1893), [Hôtel van Eetvelde](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_van_Eetvelde "Hôtel van Eetvelde") (1898), [Hôtel Solvay](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_Solvay "Hôtel Solvay") (1900) and the [Horta Museum](/wiki/Horta_Museum "Horta Museum") (1901)—have been listed as a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") since 2000.[[85]](#cite_note-:6-88) Another example of Brussels' Art Nouveau is the [Stoclet Palace](/wiki/Stoclet_Palace "Stoclet Palace") (1911), by the Viennese architect [Josef Hoffmann](/wiki/Josef_Hoffmann "Josef Hoffmann"), designated a World Heritage Site by UNESCO in June 2009.[[229]](#cite_note-234)
