[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Ancien_Institut_national_de_Radiodiffusion_-_vue_d%27ensemble.JPG/250px-Ancien_Institut_national_de_Radiodiffusion_-_vue_d%27ensemble.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ancien_Institut_national_de_Radiodiffusion_-_vue_d%27ensemble.JPG)

[Flagey Building](/wiki/Flagey_Building "Flagey Building") in [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles")

[Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco_in_Brussels "Art Deco in Brussels") structures in Brussels include the Résidence Palace (1927) (now part of the [Europa building](/wiki/Europa_building "Europa building")), the [Centre for Fine Arts](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts "Centre for Fine Arts") (1928), the [Villa Empain](/wiki/Villa_Empain "Villa Empain") (1934), the [Town Hall of Forest](/wiki/Forest_Town_Hall "Forest Town Hall") (1938), and the [Flagey Building](/wiki/Flagey_Building "Flagey Building") (also known as the *Radio House*) on the [Place Eugène Flagey/Eugène Flageyplein](/wiki/Place_Eug%C3%A8ne_Flagey "Place Eugène Flagey") (1938) in Ixelles. Some religious buildings from the [interwar era](/wiki/Interwar_era "Interwar era") were also constructed in that style, such as the [Church of St. John the Baptist](/wiki/Church_of_St._John_the_Baptist,_Molenbeek "Church of St. John the Baptist, Molenbeek") (1932) in Molenbeek and the [Church of St. Augustine](/wiki/Church_of_St._Augustine,_Forest "Church of St. Augustine, Forest") (1935) in Forest. Completed only in 1969, and combining Art Deco with [neo-Byzantine](/wiki/Neo-Byzantine "Neo-Byzantine") elements, the [Basilica of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_Sacred_Heart,_Brussels "Basilica of the Sacred Heart, Brussels") in [Koekelberg](/wiki/Koekelberg "Koekelberg") is one of the [largest churches by area in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_church_buildings_in_the_world "List of largest church buildings in the world"), and its [cupola](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") provides a panoramic view of Brussels and its outskirts. Another example are the exhibition halls of the Centenary Palace, built for the [1935 World's Fair](/wiki/Brussels_International_Exposition_(1935) "Brussels International Exposition (1935)") on the [Heysel/Heizel Plateau](/wiki/Heysel_Plateau "Heysel Plateau") in northern Brussels, home to the Brussels Exhibition Centre ([Brussels Expo](/wiki/Brussels_Expo "Brussels Expo")).[[230]](#cite_note-235)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Laeken_Atomium_06.jpg/250px-Laeken_Atomium_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laeken_Atomium_06.jpg)

The [Atomium](/wiki/Atomium "Atomium")

The [Atomium](/wiki/Atomium "Atomium") is a symbolic 103 m-tall (338 ft) [modernist](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") structure, located on the Heysel Plateau, which was originally built for the 1958 World's Fair ([Expo 58](/wiki/Expo_58 "Expo 58")). It consists of nine steel spheres connected by tubes, and forms a model of an [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron") [crystal](/wiki/Crystal "Crystal") (specifically, a [unit cell](/wiki/Unit_cell "Unit cell")), magnified 165 billion times. The architect [André Waterkeyn](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Waterkeyn "André Waterkeyn") devoted the building to science. It is now considered a [landmark](/wiki/Landmark "Landmark") of Brussels.[[231]](#cite_note-236)[[232]](#cite_note-237) Next to the Atomium, is [Mini-Europe](/wiki/Mini-Europe "Mini-Europe") [miniature park](/wiki/Miniature_park "Miniature park"), with 1:25 scale [maquettes](/wiki/Maquette "Maquette") of famous buildings from across Europe.

Since the second half of the 20th century, modern office towers have been built in Brussels ([Madou Tower](/wiki/Madou_Tower "Madou Tower"), [Rogier Tower](/wiki/Rogier_Tower "Rogier Tower"), [Proximus Towers](/wiki/Proximus_Towers "Proximus Towers"), [Finance Tower](/wiki/Finance_Tower "Finance Tower"), the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(Brussels) "World Trade Center (Brussels)"), among others). There are some thirty towers, mostly concentrated in the city's [main business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district"): the [Northern Quarter](/wiki/Northern_Quarter_(Brussels) "Northern Quarter (Brussels)") (also called *Little Manhattan*), near [Brussels-North railway station](/wiki/Brussels-North_railway_station "Brussels-North railway station"). The [South Tower](/wiki/South_Tower_(Brussels) "South Tower (Brussels)"), standing adjacent to [Brussels-South railway station](/wiki/Brussels-South_railway_station "Brussels-South railway station"), is the [tallest building in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_Belgium "List of tallest structures in Belgium"), at 148 m (486 ft). Along the [North–South connection](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_connection "North–South connection"), is the State Administrative Centre, an administrative complex in the [International Style](/wiki/International_Style "International Style"). The postmodern buildings of the [Espace Léopold](/wiki/Espace_L%C3%A9opold "Espace Léopold") complete the picture.

The city's embrace of modern architecture translated into an ambivalent approach towards historic preservation, leading to the destruction of notable architectural landmarks, most famously the [Maison du Peuple/Volkshuis](/wiki/Maison_du_Peuple,_Brussels "Maison du Peuple, Brussels") by Victor Horta, a process known as [Brusselisation](/wiki/Brusselisation "Brusselisation").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEState200451–52-97)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStubbsMakaš2011121-98)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Culture")]

Further information: [Culture of Brussels](/wiki/Culture_of_Brussels "Culture of Brussels") and [Culture of Belgium](/wiki/Culture_of_Belgium "Culture of Belgium")

### Visual arts and museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Visual arts and museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Brussels_Cinquantenaire_R03.jpg/250px-Brussels_Cinquantenaire_R03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_Cinquantenaire_R03.jpg)

[Cinquantenaire/Jubelpark](/wiki/Cinquantenaire "Cinquantenaire") [memorial arcade](/wiki/Cinquantenaire_Arcade "Cinquantenaire Arcade") and museums

Brussels [contains over 80 museums](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Brussels "List of museums in Brussels").[[233]](#cite_note-238) The [Royal Museums of Fine Arts](/wiki/Royal_Museums_of_Fine_Arts_of_Belgium "Royal Museums of Fine Arts of Belgium") has an extensive collection of various painters, such as [Flemish old masters](/wiki/Flemish_painting "Flemish painting") like [Bruegel](/wiki/Pieter_Bruegel_the_Elder "Pieter Bruegel the Elder"), [Rogier van der Weyden](/wiki/Rogier_van_der_Weyden "Rogier van der Weyden"), [Robert Campin](/wiki/Robert_Campin "Robert Campin"), [Anthony van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck"), [Jacob Jordaens](/wiki/Jacob_Jordaens "Jacob Jordaens"), and [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens"). The [Magritte Museum](/wiki/Magritte_Museum "Magritte Museum") houses the world's largest collection of works by the [surrealist](/wiki/Surrealist "Surrealist") artist [René Magritte](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Magritte "René Magritte"). Museums dedicated to the national history of Belgium include the [BELvue Museum](/wiki/BELvue_Museum "BELvue Museum"), the [Royal Museums of Art and History](/wiki/Royal_Museums_of_Art_and_History "Royal Museums of Art and History"), and the [Royal Museum of the Armed Forces and Military History](/wiki/Royal_Museum_of_the_Armed_Forces_and_Military_History "Royal Museum of the Armed Forces and Military History"). The [Musical Instruments Museum](/wiki/Musical_Instruments_Museum,_Brussels "Musical Instruments Museum, Brussels") (MIM), housed in the [Old England](/wiki/Old_England_(department_store) "Old England (department store)") building, is part of the Royal Museums of Art and History, and is internationally renowned for its collection of over 8,000 instruments.

The Brussels Museums Council is an independent body for all the museums in the Brussels-Capital Region, covering around 100 federal, private, municipal, and community museums.[[234]](#cite_note-239) It promotes member museums through the Brussels Card (giving access to public transport and 30 of the 100 museums), the Brussels Museums Nocturnes (every Thursday from 5 p.m. to 10 p.m. from mid-September to mid-December) and the Museum Night Fever (an event for and by young people on a Saturday night in late February or early March).[[235]](#cite_note-240)

Brussels has had a distinguished art scene for many years. The Belgian surrealists René Magritte and [Paul Delvaux](/wiki/Paul_Delvaux "Paul Delvaux"), for instance, studied and lived there, as did the [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") dramatist [Michel de Ghelderode](/wiki/Michel_de_Ghelderode "Michel de Ghelderode"). The city was also home of the [impressionist](/wiki/Impressionist "Impressionist") painter [Anna Boch](/wiki/Anna_Boch "Anna Boch") from the artists' group *[Les XX](/wiki/Les_XX "Les XX")*, and includes other Belgian painters such as [Léon Spilliaert](/wiki/L%C3%A9on_Spilliaert "Léon Spilliaert"). Brussels is also a capital of the [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip");[[3]](#cite_note-Comic-3) some treasured Belgian characters are *[Tintin](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Tintin "The Adventures of Tintin")*, *[Lucky Luke](/wiki/Lucky_Luke "Lucky Luke")*, *[The Smurfs](/wiki/The_Smurfs "The Smurfs")*, *[Spirou](/wiki/Spirou_et_Fantasio "Spirou et Fantasio")*, *[Gaston](/wiki/Gaston_(comics) "Gaston (comics)")*, *[Marsupilami](/wiki/Marsupilami "Marsupilami")*, *[Blake and Mortimer](/wiki/Blake_and_Mortimer "Blake and Mortimer")*, *[Boule et Bill](/wiki/Boule_et_Bill "Boule et Bill")* and *[Cubitus](/wiki/Cubitus "Cubitus")* (see [Belgian comics](/wiki/Belgian_comics "Belgian comics")). Throughout the city, walls are painted with large motifs of comic book characters; these [murals](/wiki/Mural "Mural") taken together are known as [Brussels' Comic Book Route](/wiki/Brussels%27_Comic_Book_Route "Brussels' Comic Book Route").[[46]](#cite_note-:1-49) Also, the interiors of some [Metro stations](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") are designed by artists. The [Belgian Comic Strip Center](/wiki/Belgian_Comic_Strip_Center "Belgian Comic Strip Center") combines two artistic leitmotifs of Brussels, being a museum devoted to Belgian comic strips, housed in the former *Magasins Waucquez* textile department store, designed by [Victor Horta](/wiki/Victor_Horta "Victor Horta") in the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau_in_Brussels "Art Nouveau in Brussels") style. In addition, [street art](/wiki/Street_art "Street art") is changing the landscape of this multicultural city.[[236]](#cite_note-241)

### Performing arts venues and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Performing arts venues and festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_la_Monnaie_1.JPG/250px-Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_la_Monnaie_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_de_la_Monnaie_1.JPG)

[Royal Theatre of La Monnaie](/wiki/La_Monnaie "La Monnaie")

Brussels is well known for its [performing arts](/wiki/Performing_arts "Performing arts") scene, with the [Royal Theatre of La Monnaie](/wiki/La_Monnaie "La Monnaie"), the [Royal Park Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Park_Theatre "Royal Park Theatre"), the [Théâtre Royal des Galeries](/wiki/Th%C3%A9%C3%A2tre_Royal_des_Galeries "Théâtre Royal des Galeries"), and the [Kaaitheater](/wiki/Kaaitheater "Kaaitheater") among the most notable institutions.

The [Kunstenfestivaldesarts](/wiki/Kunstenfestivaldesarts "Kunstenfestivaldesarts"), an international performing arts festival, is organised every year in May. Its main hub is the Kaaitheater, but performances and artworks are also hosted in around 30 venues throughout the city.[[237]](#cite_note-242)[[238]](#cite_note-243)

The [King Baudouin Stadium](/wiki/King_Baudouin_Stadium "King Baudouin Stadium") is a concert and competition facility with a 50,000 seat capacity, the largest in Belgium. The site was formerly occupied by the [Heysel Stadium](/wiki/Heysel_Stadium "Heysel Stadium"). The [Centre for Fine Arts](/wiki/Centre_for_Fine_Arts "Centre for Fine Arts") (often referred to as BOZAR in French or PSK in Dutch), a multi-purpose centre for theatre, cinema, music, literature, and art exhibitions, is home to the [National Orchestra of Belgium](/wiki/National_Orchestra_of_Belgium "National Orchestra of Belgium") and to the annual [Queen Elisabeth Competition](/wiki/Queen_Elisabeth_Competition "Queen Elisabeth Competition") for [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music") singers and instrumentalists, one of the most challenging and prestigious competitions of the kind. Studio 4 in [Le Flagey](/wiki/Le_Flagey "Le Flagey") cultural centre hosts the [Brussels Philharmonic](/wiki/Brussels_Philharmonic "Brussels Philharmonic").[[239]](#cite_note-244)[[240]](#cite_note-245)

Other concert venues include [Forest National/Vorst Nationaal](/wiki/Forest_National "Forest National"), the [Ancienne Belgique](/wiki/Ancienne_Belgique "Ancienne Belgique"), the [Cirque Royal/Koninklijk Circus](/wiki/Cirque_Royal "Cirque Royal"), the [Botanique](/wiki/Le_Botanique "Le Botanique") and [Palais 12/Paleis 12](/wiki/Palais_12 "Palais 12"). Furthermore, the [Jazz Station](/wiki/Jazz_Station "Jazz Station") in [Saint-Josse-ten-Noode](/wiki/Saint-Josse-ten-Noode "Saint-Josse-ten-Noode") is a museum and archive on [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), and a venue for jazz concerts.[[241]](#cite_note-246)

### Other cultural events and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Other cultural events and festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Brussels_Summer_Festival_%284890801736%29.jpg/250px-Brussels_Summer_Festival_%284890801736%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_Summer_Festival_(4890801736).jpg)

[Brussels Summer Festival](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brussels_Summer_Festival "fr:Brussels Summer Festival") (BSF)

Many events are organised or hosted in Brussels throughout the year. In addition, many festivals animate the Brussels scene.

The [Iris Festival](/wiki/Iris_Festival "Iris Festival") is the official festival of the Brussels-Capital Region and is held annually in spring.[[242]](#cite_note-247) The International Fantastic Film Festival of Brussels (BIFFF) is organised during the Easter holidays[[243]](#cite_note-248) and the [Magritte Awards](/wiki/Magritte_Award "Magritte Award") in February. The [Festival of Europe](/wiki/Festival_of_Europe "Festival of Europe"), an open day and activities in and around the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union")'s institutions, is held on 9 May. On [Belgian National Day](/wiki/Belgian_National_Day "Belgian National Day"), on 21 July, a military parade and celebrations take place on the Place des Palais/Paleizenplein and in [Brussels Park](/wiki/Brussels_Park "Brussels Park"), ending with a fireworks display in the evening.

Some summer festivities include [Couleur Café Festival](/wiki/Couleur_Caf%C3%A9_Festival "Couleur Café Festival"), a festival of [world](/wiki/World_music "World music") and [urban music](/wiki/Urban_music "Urban music"), around the end of June or early July, the [Brussels Summer Festival](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brussels_Summer_Festival "fr:Brussels Summer Festival") (BSF), a music festival in August,[[244]](#cite_note-249) the [Midi Fair](/wiki/Midi_Fair "Midi Fair"), the most important yearly fair in Brussels, lasting more than a month, in July and August,[[245]](#cite_note-250) and Brussels Beach, when the banks of the [canal](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Charleroi_Canal "Brussels–Charleroi Canal") are turned into a temporary urban beach.[[246]](#cite_note-251) Other biennial events are the [Zinneke Parade](/wiki/Zinneke_Parade "Zinneke Parade"), a colourful, multicultural parade through the city, which has been held since 2000 in May, as well as the popular [Flower Carpet](/wiki/Flower_Carpet_(Brussels) "Flower Carpet (Brussels)") at the [Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place") in August. [Heritage Days](/wiki/European_Heritage_Days "European Heritage Days") are organised on the third weekend of September (sometimes coinciding with the car-free day) and are a good opportunity to discover the wealth of buildings, institutions and real estate in Brussels. The "Winter Wonders" animate the heart of Brussels in December; these winter activities were launched in Brussels in 2001.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEState2004108-252)[[248]](#cite_note-253)

### Folklore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Folklore")]

Further information: [Folklore of Belgium](/wiki/Folklore_of_Belgium "Folklore of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/GeantsMeyboom2.jpg/250px-GeantsMeyboom2.jpg)](/wiki/File:GeantsMeyboom2.jpg)

[Meyboom](/wiki/Meyboom "Meyboom") giants in Brussels, a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Intangible Cultural Heritage](/wiki/Intangible_Cultural_Heritage "Intangible Cultural Heritage")

Brussels' identity owes much to its rich folklore and traditions, among the liveliest in the country.[[249]](#cite_note-254)[[250]](#cite_note-255)

The [Ommegang](/wiki/Ommegang_of_Brussels "Ommegang of Brussels"), a [medieval pageant](/wiki/Medieval_pageant "Medieval pageant") and folkloric costumed procession, commemorating the [Joyous Entry](/wiki/Joyous_Entry "Joyous Entry") of [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Emperor_Charles_V "Emperor Charles V") and his son, [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), in the city in 1549, takes place every year in July. The colourful parade includes floats, traditional processional giants, such as Saint [Michael](/wiki/Michael_(archangel) "Michael (archangel)") and Saint [Gudula](/wiki/Gudula "Gudula"), and scores of folkloric groups, either on foot or on horseback, dressed in medieval garb. The parade concludes with a large spectacle at the [Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place"). Since 2019, it has been recognised as a [Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/Masterpiece_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[251]](#cite_note-256)

The [Meyboom](/wiki/Meyboom "Meyboom"), an even-older folk tradition of Brussels (1308), celebrating the "[May tree](/wiki/Maypole "Maypole")"—in fact, a corruption of the Dutch word, meaning *tree of joy*—takes place paradoxically on 9 August. After parading a young [beech](/wiki/Beech "Beech") in the city, it is planted in a joyful spirit with lots of music, *Brusseleir* songs, and processional giants. It has also been recognised as an expression of [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage") by UNESCO, as part of the bi-national inscription "[Processional giants and dragons in Belgium and France](/wiki/Processional_giants_and_dragons_in_Belgium_and_France "Processional giants and dragons in Belgium and France")".[[252]](#cite_note-257)[[253]](#cite_note-258) The celebration is reminiscent of the town's long-standing (folkloric) feud with [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven"), which dates back to the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").

[Saint Verhaegen](/wiki/Saint_Verhaegen "Saint Verhaegen") (often shortened to *St V*), a folkloric student procession, celebrating the anniversary of the founding of the [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles") (ULB) and the [Vrije Universiteit Brussel](/wiki/Vrije_Universiteit_Brussel "Vrije Universiteit Brussel") (VUB), is held on 20 November. Since 2019, it has also been listed as intangible cultural heritage of the Brussels-Capital Region.[[254]](#cite_note-259)[[255]](#cite_note-260)[[256]](#cite_note-261)

Another good introduction to the *Brusseleir* [local dialect](/wiki/Brusselian_dialect "Brusselian dialect") and way of life can be obtained at the [Royal Theatre Toone](/wiki/Royal_Theatre_Toone "Royal Theatre Toone"), a folkloric theatre of [marionettes](/wiki/Marionette "Marionette"), located a stone's throw away from the Grand-Place.[[257]](#cite_note-262) Finally, two folkloric plays, *[Le Mariage de mademoiselle Beulemans](/wiki/Le_Mariage_de_mademoiselle_Beulemans "Le Mariage de mademoiselle Beulemans")* by Frantz Fonson and Fernand Wicheler, and *[Bossemans et Coppenolle](/wiki/Bossemans_et_Coppenolle "Bossemans et Coppenolle")* by Joris d'Hanswyck and Paul Van Stalle, are still the subject of regular revivals.[[258]](#cite_note-263)

## Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Cuisine of Brussels](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Brussels "Cuisine of Brussels")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Brussels_waffle.jpg/250px-Brussels_waffle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_waffle.jpg)

Brussels is known for its [local waffles](/wiki/Waffle#Brussels "Waffle").

Brussels is well known for its local [waffle](/wiki/Belgian_waffle "Belgian waffle"), its [chocolate](/wiki/Belgian_chocolate "Belgian chocolate"), its [French fries](/wiki/French_fries "French fries") and its numerous types of [beers](/wiki/Beer_in_Belgium "Beer in Belgium"). The [Brussels sprout](/wiki/Brussels_sprout "Brussels sprout"), which has long been popular in Brussels, and may have originated there, is also named after the city.[[259]](#cite_note-264)

Owing to Brussels' cosmopolitan population, almost every national cuisine in the world can be found there. The gastronomic offer includes approximately 1,800 [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant") (including three 2-starred and ten 1-starred [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide") restaurants),[[260]](#cite_note-265) and a number of bars. In addition to the traditional restaurants, there are many [cafés](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9s "Cafés"), [bistros](/wiki/Bistro "Bistro") and the usual range of international [fast food](/wiki/Fast_food_restaurant "Fast food restaurant") chains. The cafés are similar to bars, and offer beer and light dishes; [coffee houses](/wiki/Coffee_houses "Coffee houses") are called *salons de thé* (literally "tea salons"). Also widespread are [brasseries](/wiki/Brasserie "Brasserie"), which usually offer a variety of beers and typical national dishes.

[Belgian cuisine](/wiki/Belgian_cuisine "Belgian cuisine") is known among connoisseurs as one of the best in Europe. It is characterised by the combination of [French cuisine](/wiki/French_cuisine "French cuisine") with the more hearty Flemish fare. Notable specialities include [Brussels waffles](/wiki/Brussels_waffle "Brussels waffle") (gaufres) and [mussels](/wiki/Mussels "Mussels") (usually as [*moules-frites*](/wiki/Moules_frites "Moules frites"), served with fries). The city is a stronghold of [chocolate](/wiki/Belgian_chocolate "Belgian chocolate") and [praline](/wiki/Praline_(Belgian_chocolate) "Praline (Belgian chocolate)") manufacturers with renowned companies like [Côte d'Or](/wiki/C%C3%B4te_d%27Or_(chocolate) "Côte d'Or (chocolate)"), [Neuhaus](/wiki/Chocolatier_Neuhaus "Chocolatier Neuhaus"), [Leonidas](/wiki/Leonidas_(chocolate_maker) "Leonidas (chocolate maker)") and [Godiva](/wiki/Godiva_Chocolatier "Godiva Chocolatier"). Pralines were first introduced in 1912 by Jean Neuhaus II, a Belgian [chocolatier](/wiki/Chocolatier "Chocolatier") of Swiss origin, in the [Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries](/wiki/Royal_Saint-Hubert_Galleries "Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries").[[261]](#cite_note-266) Numerous [friteries](/wiki/Friteries "Friteries") are spread throughout the city, and in tourist areas, fresh hot waffles are also sold on the street.

As well as other [Belgian beers](/wiki/Beer_in_Belgium "Beer in Belgium"), the [spontaneously fermented](/wiki/Spontaneously_fermented "Spontaneously fermented") [lambic](/wiki/Lambic "Lambic") style, brewed in and around Brussels, is widely available there and in the nearby [Senne](/wiki/Senne_(river) "Senne (river)") valley where the wild [yeasts](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast") that ferment it have their origin.[[262]](#cite_note-Jackson1988-267) [Kriek](/wiki/Kriek "Kriek"), a cherry lambic, is available in almost every bar or restaurant in Brussels.

Brussels is known as the birthplace of the [Belgian endive](/wiki/Belgian_endive "Belgian endive"). The technique for growing [blanched](/wiki/Blanching_(horticulture) "Blanching (horticulture)") endives was accidentally discovered in the 1850s at the [Botanical Garden of Brussels](/wiki/Botanical_Garden_of_Brussels "Botanical Garden of Brussels") in [Saint-Josse-ten-Noode](/wiki/Saint-Josse-ten-Noode "Saint-Josse-ten-Noode").[[263]](#cite_note-268)

## Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Shopping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/107_-_March%C3%A9_aux_puces_place_du_Jeu_de_Balle_-_Bruxelles.jpg/250px-107_-_March%C3%A9_aux_puces_place_du_Jeu_de_Balle_-_Bruxelles.jpg)](/wiki/File:107_-_March%C3%A9_aux_puces_place_du_Jeu_de_Balle_-_Bruxelles.jpg)

[Flea market](/wiki/Flea_market "Flea market") on the [Place du Jeu de Balle/Vossenplein](/wiki/Place_du_Jeu_de_Balle "Place du Jeu de Balle")

Famous [shopping](/wiki/Shopping "Shopping") areas in Brussels include the pedestrian-only [Rue Neuve/Nieuwstraat](/wiki/Rue_Neuve_(Brussels) "Rue Neuve (Brussels)"), the second busiest shopping street in Belgium (after the [Meir](/wiki/Meir,_Antwerp "Meir, Antwerp"), in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")) with a weekly average of 230,000 visitors,[[264]](#cite_note-269)[[265]](#cite_note-270) home to popular international chains ([H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M"), [C&A](/wiki/C%26A "C&A"), [Zara](/wiki/Zara_(retailer) "Zara (retailer)"), [Primark](/wiki/Primark "Primark")), as well as the City 2 and Anspach galleries.[[266]](#cite_note-:3-271) The [Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries](/wiki/Royal_Saint-Hubert_Galleries "Royal Saint-Hubert Galleries") hold a variety of luxury shops and some six million people stroll through them each year.[[267]](#cite_note-272) The neighbourhood around the Rue Antoine Dansaert/Antoine Dansaertstraat has become, in recent years, a focal point for fashion and design;[[268]](#cite_note-273) this main street and its side streets also feature Belgium's young and most happening artistic talent.[[269]](#cite_note-274)

In Ixelles, the Avenue de la Toison d'Or/Gulden-Vlieslaan and the [Namur Gate](/wiki/Namur_Gate "Namur Gate") area offer a blend of luxury shops, fast food restaurants and entertainment venues, and the Chaussée d'Ixelles/Elsenesteenweg, in the mainly-Congolese *[Matongé](/wiki/Ixelles#Matongé "Ixelles")* district, offers a taste of [African fashion](/wiki/African_fashion "African fashion") and lifestyle. The nearby [Avenue Louise/Louizalaan](/wiki/Avenue_Louise/Louizalaan "Avenue Louise/Louizalaan") is lined with high-end fashion stores and boutiques, making it one of the most expensive streets in Belgium.[[270]](#cite_note-275)

There are shopping centres outside the inner ring: Basilix, Woluwe Shopping Center, Westland Shopping Center, and Docks Bruxsel, which opened in October 2017.[[266]](#cite_note-:3-271) In addition, Brussels ranks as one of Europe's best capital cities for [flea market](/wiki/Flea_market "Flea market") shopping. The *Old Market*, on the [Place du Jeu de Balle/Vossenplein](/wiki/Place_du_Jeu_de_Balle "Place du Jeu de Balle"), in the [Marolles/Marollen](/wiki/Marollen "Marollen") neighbourhood, is particularly renowned.[[271]](#cite_note-276) The nearby [Sablon/Zavel](/wiki/Sablon_(Brussels) "Sablon (Brussels)") area is home to many of Brussels' [antique](/wiki/Antique "Antique") dealers.[[272]](#cite_note-277) The *Midi Market* around [Brussels-South station](/wiki/Brussels-South_station "Brussels-South station") and the Boulevard du Midi/Zuidlaan is reputed to be one of the largest markets in Europe.[[273]](#cite_note-278)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Sports")]

Further information: [Sport in Belgium](/wiki/Sport_in_Belgium "Sport in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/20_km_de_Bruxelles.jpg/250px-20_km_de_Bruxelles.jpg)](/wiki/File:20_km_de_Bruxelles.jpg)

[20 km of Brussels](/wiki/20_km_of_Brussels "20 km of Brussels")

Sport in Brussels is under the responsibility of the [Communities](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). The *Administration de l'Éducation Physique et du Sport* ([ADEPS](/wiki/ADEPS "ADEPS")) is responsible for recognising the various French-speaking sports federations and also runs three sports centres in the Brussels-Capital Region.[[274]](#cite_note-279) Its Dutch-speaking counterpart is *Sport Vlaanderen* (formerly called [BLOSO](/wiki/Bloso "Bloso")).[[275]](#cite_note-280)

The [King Baudouin Stadium](/wiki/King_Baudouin_Stadium "King Baudouin Stadium") (formerly the Heysel Stadium) is the largest in the country and home to the national teams in [football](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") and [rugby union](/wiki/Belgium_national_rugby_union_team "Belgium national rugby union team").[[276]](#cite_note-281) It hosted the final of the [1972 UEFA European Football Championship](/wiki/1972_UEFA_European_Football_Championship "1972 UEFA European Football Championship"), and the opening game of [the 2000 edition](/wiki/Euro_2000 "Euro 2000"). Several European club finals have been held at the ground, including the [1985 European Cup Final](/wiki/1985_European_Cup_Final "1985 European Cup Final"), which saw 39 deaths due to hooliganism and structural collapse.[[277]](#cite_note-282) The King Baudouin Stadium is also home of the annual [Memorial Van Damme](/wiki/Memorial_Van_Damme "Memorial Van Damme") athletics event, Belgium's foremost [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") competition, which is part of the [Diamond League](/wiki/Diamond_League "Diamond League"). Other important athletics events are the [Brussels Marathon](/wiki/Brussels_Marathon "Brussels Marathon")[[278]](#cite_note-283) and the [20 km of Brussels](/wiki/20_km_of_Brussels "20 km of Brussels"), an annual run with 30,000 participants.[[279]](#cite_note-284)

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Lotto_park_2022.jpg/250px-Lotto_park_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lotto_park_2022.jpg)

[R.S.C. Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") fans at the [Constant Vanden Stock Stadium](/wiki/Constant_Vanden_Stock_Stadium "Constant Vanden Stock Stadium")

[R.S.C. Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht"), based in the [Constant Vanden Stock Stadium](/wiki/Constant_Vanden_Stock_Stadium "Constant Vanden Stock Stadium") in [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht"), is the most successful Belgian football club in the [Belgian Pro League](/wiki/Belgian_Pro_League "Belgian Pro League"), with 34 titles.[[280]](#cite_note-285) It has also won the most major European tournaments for a Belgian side, with 6 European titles. Brussels is also home to [Union Saint-Gilloise](/wiki/Union_Saint-Gilloise "Union Saint-Gilloise"), the most successful Belgian club before World War II, with 11 titles.[[281]](#cite_note-286) The club was founded in [Saint-Gilles](/wiki/Saint-Gilles,_Belgium "Saint-Gilles, Belgium") but is based in nearby [Forest](/wiki/Forest,_Belgium "Forest, Belgium"), and plays in the Belgian Pro League. [Racing White Daring Molenbeek](/wiki/RWDM47 "RWDM47"), based in [Molenbeek-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Molenbeek-Saint-Jean "Molenbeek-Saint-Jean"), and often referred to as RWDM, is a very popular football club that, since 2023, is back playing in the Belgian Pro League.[[282]](#cite_note-287)[[283]](#cite_note-288)

Other Brussels clubs that played in the national series over the years were [Royal White Star Bruxelles](/wiki/Royal_White_Star_Bruxelles "Royal White Star Bruxelles"), Ixelles SC, [Crossing Club de Schaerbeek](/wiki/Crossing_Club_de_Schaerbeek "Crossing Club de Schaerbeek") (born from a merger between RCS de Schaerbeek and Crossing Club Molenbeek), Scup Jette, RUS de Laeken, Racing Jet de Bruxelles, AS Auderghem, KV Wosjot Woluwe and FC Ganshoren.

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Brussels is home to notable [cycling races](/wiki/Cycling_race "Cycling race"). The city is the arrival location of the [Brussels Cycling Classic](/wiki/Brussels_Cycling_Classic "Brussels Cycling Classic"), formerly known as Paris–Brussels, which is one of the oldest [semi classic](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") [bicycle races](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing") on the international calendar.[[284]](#cite_note-289) From World War I until the early 1970s, the [Six Days of Brussels](/wiki/Six_Days_of_Brussels "Six Days of Brussels") was organised regularly. In the last decades of the 20th century, the [Grand Prix Eddy Merckx](/wiki/Grand_Prix_Eddy_Merckx "Grand Prix Eddy Merckx") was also held in Brussels.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Economy")]

Further information: [Economy of Belgium](/wiki/Economy_of_Belgium "Economy of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Front_of_Brussels_Stock_Exchange_2023_cropped.jpg/250px-Front_of_Brussels_Stock_Exchange_2023_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_of_Brussels_Stock_Exchange_2023_cropped.jpg)

The [Bourse Palace](/wiki/Bourse_Palace "Bourse Palace"), the former [Brussels Stock Exchange](/wiki/Brussels_Stock_Exchange "Brussels Stock Exchange") (BSE) building

Serving as the centre of administration for Belgium and Europe, Brussels' economy is largely [service](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy")-oriented. It is dominated by regional and world headquarters of [multinationals](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"), by European institutions, by various local and federal administrations, and by related services companies, though it does have a number of notable [craft](/wiki/Craft "Craft") industries, such as the [Cantillon Brewery](/wiki/Cantillon_Brewery "Cantillon Brewery"), a [lambic](/wiki/Lambic "Lambic") brewery founded in 1900.[[285]](#cite_note-290)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/North_Galaxy_Towers-Schaerbeek-001.JPG/250px-North_Galaxy_Towers-Schaerbeek-001.JPG)](/wiki/File:North_Galaxy_Towers-Schaerbeek-001.JPG)

Brussels' [Northern Quarter](/wiki/Northern_Quarter_(Brussels) "Northern Quarter (Brussels)") business district

Brussels has a robust economy. The region contributes to one fifth of Belgium's [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP"), and its 550,000 jobs account for 17.7% of Belgium's employment.[[286]](#cite_note-291) Its [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") is nearly double that of Belgium as a whole,[[25]](#cite_note-:4-28) and it has the highest GDP per capita of any [NUTS 1](/wiki/Nomenclature_of_Territorial_Units_for_Statistics "Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics") region in the EU, at ~$80,000 in 2016.[[287]](#cite_note-292) However, the GDP is boosted by a massive inflow of [commuters](/wiki/Commuters "Commuters") from neighbouring regions; over half of those who work in Brussels live in Flanders or Wallonia, with 230,000 and 130,000 commuters per day respectively. Conversely, only 16.0% of Brussels residents work outside the region (68,827 (68.5%) of them in Flanders and 21,035 (31.5%) in Wallonia).[[288]](#cite_note-293) Not all of the wealth generated in Brussels remains in the region, and as of December 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit), the unemployment among its residents is 20.4%.[[289]](#cite_note-294)

There are approximately 50,000 [businesses](/wiki/Business "Business") in Brussels, of which around 2,200 are foreign. This number is constantly increasing and can well explain the role of Brussels in Europe. The city's [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") is very favourable in terms of starting up a new business. House prices have also increased in recent years, especially with the increase of young [professionals](/wiki/Professional "Professional") settling down in Brussels, making it the most expensive city to live in Belgium.[[290]](#cite_note-295) In addition, Brussels holds more than 1,000 business conferences annually, making it the ninth most popular conference city in Europe.[[291]](#cite_note-296)

Brussels is rated as the 34th most important financial centre in the world as of 2020, according to the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index"). The [Brussels Stock Exchange](/wiki/Brussels_Stock_Exchange "Brussels Stock Exchange"), abbreviated to BSE and now called Euronext Brussels, is part of the European stock exchange [Euronext](/wiki/Euronext "Euronext"), along with [Paris Bourse](/wiki/Paris_Bourse "Paris Bourse"), [Lisbon Stock Exchange](/wiki/Lisbon_Stock_Exchange "Lisbon Stock Exchange") and [Amsterdam Stock Exchange](/wiki/Amsterdam_Stock_Exchange "Amsterdam Stock Exchange"). Its benchmark [stock market index](/wiki/Stock_market_index "Stock market index") is the [BEL20](/wiki/BEL20 "BEL20").

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media")]

Further information: [Belgian media](/wiki/Belgian_media "Belgian media")

Brussels is a centre of both media and communications in Belgium, with many Belgian television stations, radio stations, newspapers and telephone companies having their headquarters in the region. The French-language public broadcaster [RTBF](/wiki/RTBF "RTBF"), the Dutch-language public broadcaster [VRT](/wiki/Vlaamse_Radio-_en_Televisieomroeporganisatie "Vlaamse Radio- en Televisieomroeporganisatie"), the two regional channels BX1 (formerly Télé Bruxelles)[[292]](#cite_note-297) and [Bruzz](/wiki/Bruzz "Bruzz") (formerly TV Brussel),[[293]](#cite_note-298) the encrypted [BeTV](/wiki/BeTV_(Belgium) "BeTV (Belgium)") channel and private channels [RTL-TVI](/wiki/RTL-TVI "RTL-TVI") and [VTM](/wiki/VTM_(TV_channel) "VTM (TV channel)") are headquartered in Brussels. Some national newspapers such as *[Le Soir](/wiki/Le_Soir "Le Soir")*, *[La Libre](/wiki/La_Libre "La Libre")*, *[De Morgen](/wiki/De_Morgen "De Morgen")* and the news agency [Belga](/wiki/Belga_(news_agency) "Belga (news agency)") are based in or around Brussels. The Belgian postal company [bpost](/wiki/Bpost "Bpost"), as well as the telecommunication companies and mobile operators [Proximus](/wiki/Proximus "Proximus"), [Orange Belgium](/wiki/Orange_Belgium "Orange Belgium") and [Telenet](/wiki/Telenet_(Belgium) "Telenet (Belgium)") are all located there.

As English is spoken widely,[[42]](#cite_note-BruS13EN-45)[[43]](#cite_note-:7-46) several English media organisations operate in Brussels. The most popular of these are the English-language daily news media platform and bi-monthly magazine *[The Brussels Times](/wiki/The_Brussels_Times "The Brussels Times")* and the website *[The Bulletin](/wiki/The_Bulletin_(Brussels_weekly) "The Bulletin (Brussels weekly)")*. The multilingual [pan-European](/wiki/Pan-European_identity "Pan-European identity") news channel [Euronews](/wiki/Euronews "Euronews") also maintains an office in Brussels.[[294]](#cite_note-299)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Education")]

Further information: [Education in Belgium](/wiki/Education_in_Belgium "Education in Belgium")

### Tertiary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Tertiary education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Universit%C3%A9_Libre_de_Bruxelles_Franklin_Rooseveltlaan_Brussel_01.jpg/250px-Universit%C3%A9_Libre_de_Bruxelles_Franklin_Rooseveltlaan_Brussel_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universit%C3%A9_Libre_de_Bruxelles_Franklin_Rooseveltlaan_Brussel_01.jpg)

[Solbosch/Solbos](/wiki/Neighbourhoods_in_Brussels#Solbosch/Solbos "Neighbourhoods in Brussels") campus of the [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles") (ULB)

There are several [universities](/wiki/Universities "Universities") in Brussels. Except for the [Royal Military Academy](/wiki/Royal_Military_Academy_(Belgium) "Royal Military Academy (Belgium)"), a federal military college established in 1834,[[295]](#cite_note-300) all universities in Brussels are private and autonomous. The Royal Military Academy is also the only Belgian university organised on the [boarding school](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school") model.[[296]](#cite_note-301)

The [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles") (ULB), a French-speaking university, with about 20,000 students, has three campuses in the city,[[297]](#cite_note-302) and the [Vrije Universiteit Brussel](/wiki/Vrije_Universiteit_Brussel "Vrije Universiteit Brussel") (VUB), its [Dutch-speaking](/wiki/Dutch-speaking "Dutch-speaking") sister university, has about 10,000 students.[[298]](#cite_note-303) Both universities originate from a single ancestor university, founded in 1834, namely the [Free University of Brussels](/wiki/Free_University_of_Brussels_(1834%E2%80%931969) "Free University of Brussels (1834–1969)"), which was split in 1970, at about the same time the Flemish and French Communities gained legislative power over the organisation of higher education.[[299]](#cite_note-304)

[Saint-Louis University, Brussels](/wiki/Saint-Louis_University,_Brussels "Saint-Louis University, Brussels") (also known as UCLouvain Saint-Louis – Bruxelles) was founded in 1858 and is specialised in social and [human sciences](/wiki/Human_science "Human science"), with 4,000 students, and located on two campuses in the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") and [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles").[[300]](#cite_note-305) From September 2018 on, the university uses the name *UCLouvain*, together with the [Catholic University of Louvain](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_catholique_de_Louvain "Université catholique de Louvain"), in the context of a merger between both universities.[[301]](#cite_note-306)

Still other universities have campuses in Brussels, such as the French-speaking Catholic University of Louvain (UCLouvain), which has 10,000 students in the city with its medical faculties at [UCLouvain Bruxelles Woluwe](/wiki/UCLouvain_Bruxelles_Woluwe "UCLouvain Bruxelles Woluwe") since 1973,[[302]](#cite_note-307) in addition to its [Faculty of Architecture, Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning](/wiki/Faculty_of_Architecture,_Architectural_Engineering_and_Urban_Planning "Faculty of Architecture, Architectural Engineering and Urban Planning")[[303]](#cite_note-308) and UCLouvain's Dutch-speaking sister [Katholieke Universiteit Leuven](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit Leuven") (KU Leuven)[[304]](#cite_note-309) (offering bachelor's and master's degrees in economics & business, law, arts, and architecture; 4,400 students). In addition, the University of Kent's [Brussels School of International Studies](/wiki/Brussels_School_of_International_Studies "Brussels School of International Studies") is a specialised postgraduate school offering advanced international studies.

Also a dozen of university colleges are located in Brussels, including two drama schools, founded in 1832: the French-speaking [Conservatoire Royal](/wiki/Conservatoire_royal_de_Bruxelles "Conservatoire royal de Bruxelles") and its [Dutch-speaking](/wiki/Dutch-speaking "Dutch-speaking") equivalent, the [Koninklijk Conservatorium](/wiki/Koninklijk_Conservatorium_(Brussel) "Koninklijk Conservatorium (Brussel)").[[305]](#cite_note-310)[[306]](#cite_note-311)

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

Most of Brussels pupils between the ages of 3 and 18 go to schools organised by the [French-speaking Community](/wiki/French-speaking_Community "French-speaking Community") or the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community"), with close to 80% going to French-speaking schools, and roughly 20% to Dutch-speaking schools. Due to the post-war international presence in the city, there are also a number of international schools, including the [International School of Brussels](/wiki/International_School_of_Brussels "International School of Brussels"), with 1,450 pupils, between the ages of 2+1⁄2 and 18,[[307]](#cite_note-312) the [British School of Brussels](/wiki/British_School_of_Brussels "British School of Brussels"), and the four [European Schools](/wiki/European_School "European School"), which provide free education for the children of those working in the [EU institutions](/wiki/EU_institutions "EU institutions"). The combined student population of the four European Schools in Brussels is around 10,000.[[308]](#cite_note-313)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Libraries")]

Main article: [List of libraries in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_Belgium "List of libraries in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Albertine_-_01.jpg/250px-Albertine_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Albertine_-_01.jpg)

[Royal Library of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Library_of_Belgium "Royal Library of Belgium") (KBR)

Brussels has a number of public or private-owned [libraries](/wiki/Libraries "Libraries") on its territory.[[309]](#cite_note-314) Most public libraries in Brussels fall under the competence of the Communities and are usually separated between French-speaking and Dutch-speaking institutions, although some are mixed.[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The [Royal Library of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Library_of_Belgium "Royal Library of Belgium") (KBR) is the [national library](/wiki/National_library "National library") of Belgium and one of the most prestigious libraries in the world. It owns several collections of historical importance, like the [Fétis](/wiki/F%C3%A9tis "Fétis") archives, and is the depository for all books ever published in Belgium or abroad by Belgian authors. It is located on the [Mont des Arts/Kunstberg](/wiki/Mont_des_Arts "Mont des Arts") in central Brussels, near the [Central Station](/wiki/Brussels_Central_Station "Brussels Central Station").[[310]](#cite_note-315)

There are several academic libraries and archives in Brussels. The libraries of the [Université libre de Bruxelles](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_libre_de_Bruxelles "Université libre de Bruxelles") (ULB) and the [Vrije Universiteit Brussel](/wiki/Vrije_Universiteit_Brussel "Vrije Universiteit Brussel") (VUB) constitute the largest ensemble of university libraries in the city. In addition to the Solbosch location, there are branches in [La Plaine/Het Plein](/wiki/Neighbourhoods_in_Brussels#La_Plaine/Het_Plein "Neighbourhoods in Brussels") and [Erasme/Erasmus](/wiki/Erasmus_metro_station "Erasmus metro station").[[311]](#cite_note-316)[[312]](#cite_note-317) Other academic libraries include those of [Saint-Louis University, Brussels](/wiki/Saint-Louis_University,_Brussels "Saint-Louis University, Brussels")[[313]](#cite_note-318) and the [Catholic University of Louvain](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_catholique_de_Louvain "Université catholique de Louvain") (UCLouvain).[[314]](#cite_note-319)

### Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Main article: [Science and technology in Brussels](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Brussels "Science and technology in Brussels")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Planetarium_Royal_Observatory_Belgium.jpg/250px-Planetarium_Royal_Observatory_Belgium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Planetarium_Royal_Observatory_Belgium.jpg)

[Planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium_(Belgium) "Planetarium (Belgium)") of the [Royal Observatory of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Observatory_of_Belgium "Royal Observatory of Belgium")

Science and technology in Brussels is well-developed with the presence of several [universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Belgium "List of universities in Belgium") and research institutes. The Brussels-Capital Region is home to several national science and technology institutes including the [National Fund for Scientific Research](/wiki/National_Fund_for_Scientific_Research "National Fund for Scientific Research") (NFSR), the Institute for the Encouragement of Scientific Research and Innovation of Brussels (ISRIB), the [Royal Academies for Science and the Arts of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Academies_for_Science_and_the_Arts_of_Belgium "Royal Academies for Science and the Arts of Belgium") (RASAB) and the [Belgian Academy Council of Applied Sciences](/wiki/Belgian_Academy_Council_of_Applied_Sciences "Belgian Academy Council of Applied Sciences") (BACAS). Several science parks associated with the universities are also spread over the region.

The [Royal Belgian Institute of Natural Sciences](/wiki/Royal_Belgian_Institute_of_Natural_Sciences "Royal Belgian Institute of Natural Sciences"), located in [Leopold Park](/wiki/Leopold_Park "Leopold Park"), houses the world's largest hall completely dedicated to [dinosaurs](/wiki/Dinosaur "Dinosaur"), with its collection of 30 [fossilised](/wiki/Fossil "Fossil") *[Iguanodon](/wiki/Iguanodon "Iguanodon")* skeletons.[[315]](#cite_note-320) In addition, the [Planetarium](/wiki/Planetarium_(Belgium) "Planetarium (Belgium)") of the [Royal Observatory of Belgium](/wiki/Royal_Observatory_of_Belgium "Royal Observatory of Belgium") (part of the institutions of the [Belgian Federal Science Policy Office](/wiki/Belgian_Federal_Science_Policy_Office "Belgian Federal Science Policy Office")), on the [Heysel Plateau](/wiki/Heysel_Plateau "Heysel Plateau") in [Laeken](/wiki/Laeken "Laeken"), is one of the largest in Europe.[[316]](#cite_note-321)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Main article: [List of hospitals in Belgium § Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Belgium#Brussels-Capital_Region "List of hospitals in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/H%C3%B4pital_Erasme_Haupteingang.jpg/250px-H%C3%B4pital_Erasme_Haupteingang.jpg)](/wiki/File:H%C3%B4pital_Erasme_Haupteingang.jpg)

[Erasmus Hospital](/wiki/Erasmus_Hospital "Erasmus Hospital") in [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht")

Brussels is home to a thriving [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry") and [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") industry, which includes pioneering [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") research. The health sector employs 70,000 employees in 30,000 companies. There are 3,000 life sciences researchers in the city and two large [science parks](/wiki/Science_park "Science park"): Da Vinci Research Park and Erasmus Research Park. There are five [university hospitals](/wiki/University_hospitals "University hospitals"), a [military hospital](/wiki/Military_hospital "Military hospital") and more than 40 general hospitals and specialist clinics.[[317]](#cite_note-322)

Due to [its bilingual nature](/wiki/Languages_of_Belgium "Languages of Belgium"), hospitals in the Brussels-Capital Region can be either monolingual French, monolingual Dutch, or bilingual, depending on their nature. University hospitals belong to one of the two [linguistic communities](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium") and are thus monolingual French or Dutch by law. Other hospitals managed by a public authority must be legally bilingual. Private hospitals are legally not bound to either language, but most cater to both. However, all hospital emergency services in the Capital Region (whether part of a public or private hospital) are required to be bilingual, since patients transported by emergency [ambulance](/wiki/Ambulance "Ambulance") cannot choose the hospital they will be brought to.[[318]](#cite_note-323)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Brussels](/wiki/Transport_in_Brussels "Transport in Brussels")

Brussels has an extensive network of both private or public transportation means. Public transportation includes [Brussels buses](/wiki/Brussels_buses "Brussels buses"), [trams](/wiki/Brussels_trams "Brussels trams"), and [metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") (all three operated by the [Brussels Intercommunal Transport Company](/wiki/Brussels_Intercommunal_Transport_Company "Brussels Intercommunal Transport Company") (STIB/MIVB)), as well as a set of railway lines (operated by [Infrabel](/wiki/Infrabel "Infrabel")) and railway stations served by public trains (operated by the [National Railway Company of Belgium](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium") (NMBS/SNCB)). Air transport is available via one of the city's two airports ([Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") and [Brussels South Charleroi Airport](/wiki/Brussels_South_Charleroi_Airport "Brussels South Charleroi Airport")), and boat transport is available via the [Port of Brussels](/wiki/Port_of_Brussels "Port of Brussels"). Bicycle-sharing and car-sharing public systems are also available.

The complexity of the Belgian political landscape makes some transportation issues difficult to solve. The Brussels-Capital Region is surrounded by the [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") and [Walloon](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") regions, which means that the airports, as well as many roads serving Brussels (most notably the [Brussels Ring](/wiki/Brussels_Ring "Brussels Ring")), are located in the other two Belgian regions. The city is relatively car-dependent by northern European standards and is considered to be the most congested city in the world according to the [INRIX](/wiki/INRIX "INRIX") traffic survey.[[319]](#cite_note-auto-324)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Brussels_Airlines_A319-112_%28OO-SSG%29_landing_at_Brussels_Airport_%281%29.jpg/250px-Brussels_Airlines_A319-112_%28OO-SSG%29_landing_at_Brussels_Airport_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_Airlines_A319-112_(OO-SSG)_landing_at_Brussels_Airport_(1).jpg)

[Brussels Airlines](/wiki/Brussels_Airlines "Brussels Airlines") [Airbus](/wiki/Airbus "Airbus") A319 landing at [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport") in [Zaventem](/wiki/Zaventem "Zaventem")

The Brussels-Capital Region is served by two airports, both located outside of the region's administrative territory. The most notable is [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport"), located in the nearby Flemish municipality of [Zaventem](/wiki/Zaventem "Zaventem"), 12 km (10 mi) east of the capital, which can be accessed by motorway (A201), train, taxi and bus. Brussels National Airport has its own railway station. This station is located in the underground (level -1) of the airport terminal building itself. The secondary airport is [Brussels South Charleroi Airport](/wiki/Brussels_South_Charleroi_Airport "Brussels South Charleroi Airport"), located in [Gosselies](/wiki/Gosselies "Gosselies"), a part of the city of [Charleroi](/wiki/Charleroi "Charleroi") ([Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")), some 50 km (30 mi) south-west of Brussels, which can be accessed by motorway (E19 then E420) or a private bus. There is also [Melsbroek Air Base](/wiki/Melsbroek_Air_Base "Melsbroek Air Base"), located in [Steenokkerzeel](/wiki/Steenokkerzeel "Steenokkerzeel"), a military airport that shares its infrastructure with Brussels Airport. The aforementioned airports are also the main airports of Belgium.[[320]](#cite_note-325)

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Water")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Bassin_de_Biestebroeck_%28DSC_4024%29.jpg/250px-Bassin_de_Biestebroeck_%28DSC_4024%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bassin_de_Biestebroeck_(DSC_4024).jpg)

View along the [Brussels–Charleroi Canal](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Charleroi_Canal "Brussels–Charleroi Canal") in [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht")

Since the 16th century, Brussels has had its own harbour, the [Port of Brussels](/wiki/Port_of_Brussels "Port of Brussels"). It has been enlarged throughout the centuries to become the second Belgian inland port. Historically situated near the [Place Sainte-Catherine/Sint-Katelijneplein](/wiki/Place_Sainte-Catherine "Place Sainte-Catherine"), it lies today to the north-west of the region, on the [Brussels–Scheldt Maritime Canal](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Scheldt_Maritime_Canal "Brussels–Scheldt Maritime Canal") (commonly called Willebroek Canal), which connects Brussels to [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") via the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"). Ships and large barges up to 4,500 t (9,900,000 lb) can penetrate deep into the country, avoiding break-ups and load transfers between Antwerp and the centre of Brussels, hence reducing the cost for companies using the canal, and thus offering a competitive advantage.

Moreover, the connection of the Willebroek Canal with the [Brussels–Charleroi Canal](/wiki/Brussels%E2%80%93Charleroi_Canal "Brussels–Charleroi Canal"), in the very heart of the capital, creates a north–south link, by means of waterways, between the Netherlands, Flanders and the industrial zone of [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_(province) "Hainaut (province)") ([Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia")). There, navigation can access the network of French canals, thanks to the important [inclined plane](/wiki/Canal_inclined_plane "Canal inclined plane") of [Ronquières](/wiki/Ronqui%C3%A8res_inclined_plane "Ronquières inclined plane") and the lifts of [Strépy-Bracquegnies](/wiki/Str%C3%A9py-Bracquegnies "Strépy-Bracquegnies").

The importance of river traffic in Brussels makes it possible to avoid the road equivalent of 740,000 trucks per year—almost 2,000 per day—which, in addition to easing traffic problems, represents an estimated carbon dioxide saving of 51,545 tonnes (113,637,000 lb) per year.[[321]](#cite_note-326)

### Train

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Train")]

Further information: [List of railway stations in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_railway_stations_in_Belgium "List of railway stations in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Station_Brussel-Zuid_Hoofdgang.jpg/250px-Station_Brussel-Zuid_Hoofdgang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Station_Brussel-Zuid_Hoofdgang.jpg)

Main hall of [Brussels-South railway station](/wiki/Brussels-South_railway_station "Brussels-South railway station"), home to the [Eurostar](/wiki/Eurostar "Eurostar") train service to London

The Brussels-Capital Region has three main train stations: [Brussels-South](/wiki/Brussels-South_railway_station "Brussels-South railway station"), [Brussels-Central](/wiki/Brussels-Central "Brussels-Central") and [Brussels-North](/wiki/Brussels-North_railway_station "Brussels-North railway station"), which are also the busiest of the country.[[37]](#cite_note-:2-40) Brussels-South is also served by direct high-speed rail links: to London by [Eurostar](/wiki/Eurostar "Eurostar") trains via the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel"); to [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam")[[322]](#cite_note-327) by Eurostar "Red" (previously [Thalys](/wiki/Thalys "Thalys")) and *[InterCity](/wiki/InterCity "InterCity")* connections; to Amsterdam, Paris, and [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") by Eurostar; and to Cologne and [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") by the German [ICE](/wiki/Intercity_Express "Intercity Express").

The train rails in Brussels go underground, near the centre, through the [North–South connection](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_connection "North–South connection"), with Brussels Central Station also being largely underground. The tunnel itself is only six tracks wide at its narrowest point, which often causes congestion and delays due to heavy use of the route.

The [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") has minor railway stations at [Bockstael](/wiki/Bockstael_railway_station "Bockstael railway station"), [Brussels-Chapel](/wiki/Brussels-Chapel_railway_station "Brussels-Chapel railway station"), [Brussels-Congress](/wiki/Brussels-Congress_railway_station "Brussels-Congress railway station"), [Brussels-Luxembourg](/wiki/Brussels-Luxembourg_railway_station "Brussels-Luxembourg railway station"), [Brussels-Schuman](/wiki/Schuman_station "Schuman station"), [Brussels-West](/wiki/Brussels-West_station "Brussels-West station"), [Haren](/wiki/Haren_railway_station_(Brussels) "Haren railway station (Brussels)"), [Haren-South](/wiki/Haren-South_railway_station "Haren-South railway station") and [Simonis](/wiki/Simonis_metro_station "Simonis metro station"). In the Brussels Region, there are also railways stations at [Berchem-Sainte-Agathe](/wiki/Berchem-Sainte-Agathe_railway_station "Berchem-Sainte-Agathe railway station"), [Boitsfort](/wiki/Boitsfort_railway_station "Boitsfort railway station"), [Boondael](/wiki/Boondael_railway_station "Boondael railway station"), [Bordet (Evere)](/wiki/Bordet_railway_station "Bordet railway station"), [Etterbeek](/wiki/Etterbeek_railway_station "Etterbeek railway station"), [Evere](/wiki/Evere_railway_station "Evere railway station"), [Forest-East](/wiki/Forest-East_railway_station "Forest-East railway station"), [Forest-South](/wiki/Forest-South_railway_station "Forest-South railway station"), [Jette](/wiki/Jette_railway_station "Jette railway station"), [Meiser (Schaerbeek)](/wiki/Meiser_railway_station "Meiser railway station"), [Moensberg (Uccle)](/wiki/Moensberg_railway_station "Moensberg railway station"), [Saint-Job (Uccle)](/wiki/Saint-Job_railway_station "Saint-Job railway station"), [Schaarbeek](/wiki/Schaerbeek_railway_station "Schaerbeek railway station"), [Uccle-Calevoet](/wiki/Uccle-Calevoet_railway_station "Uccle-Calevoet railway station"), [Uccle-Stalle](/wiki/Uccle-Stalle_railway_station "Uccle-Stalle railway station"), Vivier d'Oie-Diesdelle (Uccle), [Merode](/wiki/Merode_station "Merode station") and [Watermael](/wiki/Watermael_railway_station "Watermael railway station").

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Public transport")]

The [Brussels Intercommunal Transport Company](/wiki/Brussels_Intercommunal_Transport_Company "Brussels Intercommunal Transport Company") (STIB/MIVB) is the local [public transport](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport") operator in Brussels. It covers the 19 municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region and some surface routes extend to the near suburbs in the other two regions, linking with the [De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn") network in [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") and the [TEC](/wiki/TEC_(transport) "TEC (transport)") network in [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia").

#### Metro, trams and buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Metro, trams and buses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Rame_%22Boa%22.jpg/250px-Rame_%22Boa%22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rame_%22Boa%22.jpg)

[Brussels Metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") carriage at [Erasme/Erasmus metro station](/wiki/Erasme/Erasmus_metro_station "Erasme/Erasmus metro station")

The [Brussels Metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro") dates back to 1976,[[323]](#cite_note-328) but underground lines known as the *[premetro](/wiki/Brussels_premetro "Brussels premetro")* have been serviced by tramways since 1968. It is the only [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in Belgium ([Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_Pre-metro "Antwerp Pre-metro") and [Charleroi](/wiki/M%C3%A9tro_L%C3%A9ger_de_Charleroi "Métro Léger de Charleroi") both having [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") systems). The network consists of four conventional metro lines and three *premetro* lines. The metro-grade lines are [M1](/wiki/Brussels_Metro_line_1 "Brussels Metro line 1"), [M2](/wiki/Brussels_Metro_line_2 "Brussels Metro line 2"), [M5](/wiki/Brussels_Metro_line_5 "Brussels Metro line 5"), and [M6](/wiki/Brussels_Metro_line_6 "Brussels Metro line 6"), with some shared sections, covering a total of 40 km (25 mi).[[324]](#cite_note-The_network_and_vehicles-329) As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit), the Metro network within the region has a total of [69 metro and *premetro* stations](/wiki/List_of_Brussels_Metro_stations "List of Brussels Metro stations"). The Metro is an important [means of transport](/wiki/Transport_in_Brussels "Transport in Brussels"), connecting with six [railway stations](/wiki/Railway_station "Railway station") of the [National Railway Company of Belgium](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium") (NMBS/SNCB), and many tram and bus stops operated by STIB/MIVB, as well as with [Flemish](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders") [De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn") and [Walloon](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") [TEC](/wiki/TEC_(transport) "TEC (transport)") bus stops.

A comprehensive [bus](/wiki/Brussels_buses "Brussels buses") and [tram network](/wiki/Brussels_trams "Brussels trams") covers the region. As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit), the Brussels tram system consists of 17 tram lines (three of which – lines [T4](/wiki/Brussels_tram_route_4 "Brussels tram route 4"), [T7](/wiki/Brussels_tram_route_7 "Brussels tram route 7") and T10 – qualify as *premetro* lines that partly travel over underground sections that were intended to be eventually converted into metro lines).[[325]](#cite_note-newscientist-330) The total route length is 139 km (86 mi),[[324]](#cite_note-The_network_and_vehicles-329) making it one of the largest tram networks in Europe. The Brussels bus network is complementary to the rail network. It consists of 50 bus routes and 11 night routes, spanning 445 km (277 mi).[[324]](#cite_note-The_network_and_vehicles-329)

Since April 2007, STIB/MIVB has also been operating a night bus network called Noctis on Friday and Saturday nights from midnight until 3 a.m.[[326]](#cite_note-noctisbrus-331) The service consists of 11 routes (N04, N05, N06, N08, N09, N10, N11, N12, N13, N16 and N18).[[327]](#cite_note-332) The fare on these night buses is the same as during the day. All the lines leave from the [Place de la Bourse/Beursplein](/wiki/Place_de_la_Bourse,_Brussels "Place de la Bourse, Brussels") in the city centre at 30 minutes intervals and cover all the main streets in the capital, as they radiate outwards to the suburbs.[[328]](#cite_note-333) Noctis services returned from 2 July 2021 after over a year of disruption due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Belgium](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Belgium "COVID-19 pandemic in Belgium").[[326]](#cite_note-noctisbrus-331)

#### Other public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Other public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/VilloStationAlmostFull.jpg/250px-VilloStationAlmostFull.jpg)](/wiki/File:VilloStationAlmostFull.jpg)

[Villo!](/wiki/Villo! "Villo!") [shared bicycles](/wiki/Shared_bicycle "Shared bicycle") in Brussels

Since 2003, Brussels has had a car-sharing service operated by the [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen "Bremen") company Cambio, in partnership with STIB/MIVB and the local ridesharing company Taxi Stop.[[329]](#cite_note-334) In 2006, a [public bicycle-sharing programme](/wiki/Community_bicycle_program "Community bicycle program") was introduced. The scheme was subsequently taken over by [Villo!](/wiki/Villo! "Villo!"). Since 2008, this night-time public transport service has been supplemented by Collecto, a shared taxi system, which operates on weekdays between 11 p.m. and 6 a.m.[[330]](#cite_note-335) In 2012, the Zen Car electric car-sharing scheme was launched in the university and European areas, though it ceased operating in the city in 2020.[[331]](#cite_note-336)

### Road network

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Road network")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Wetstraat.jpg/250px-Wetstraat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wetstraat.jpg)

The [Rue de la Loi/Wetstraat](/wiki/Rue_de_la_Loi "Rue de la Loi") is one of the city's main streets.

In medieval times, Brussels stood at the intersection of routes running north–south (the modern Rue Haute/Hoogstraat) and east–west (Chaussée de Gand/Gentsesteenweg–Rue du Marché aux Herbes/Grasmarkt–Rue de Namur/Naamsestraat). The ancient pattern of streets, radiating from the [Grand-Place](/wiki/Grand-Place "Grand-Place"), in large part remains, but has been overlaid by boulevards built [over the river Senne](/wiki/Covering_of_the_Senne "Covering of the Senne"), [over the city walls](/wiki/Fortifications_of_Brussels#Construction_of_the_Small_Ring "Fortifications of Brussels") and over the railway connection between the North and South Stations. In 2012, Brussels had the most congested traffic in Europe and North America, according to US traffic information platform [INRIX](/wiki/INRIX "INRIX").[[332]](#cite_note-337)

Brussels is the hub of a range of national roads, the main ones being clockwise: the [N1](/wiki/N1_road_(Belgium) "N1 road (Belgium)") (N to [Breda](/wiki/Breda "Breda")), [N2](/wiki/N2_road_(Belgium) "N2 road (Belgium)") (E to [Maastricht](/wiki/Maastricht "Maastricht")), [N3](/wiki/N3_road_(Belgium) "N3 road (Belgium)") (E to [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen")), [N4](/wiki/N4_road_(Belgium) "N4 road (Belgium)") (SE to [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City")), [N5](/wiki/N5_road_(Belgium) "N5 road (Belgium)") (S to [Reims](/wiki/Reims "Reims")), N6 (S to [Maubeuge](/wiki/Maubeuge "Maubeuge")), N7 (SW to [Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille")), [N8](/wiki/N8_road_(Belgium) "N8 road (Belgium)") (W to [Koksijde](/wiki/Koksijde "Koksijde")) and N9 (NW to [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend "Ostend")).[[333]](#cite_note-338) Usually named *chaussées*/*steenwegen*, these roads normally run in a straight line, but sometimes lose themselves in a maze of narrow shopping streets. The region is skirted by the [European route E19](/wiki/European_route_E19 "European route E19") (N-S) and the [E40](/wiki/European_route_E40 "European route E40") (E-W), while the [E411](/wiki/E411 "E411") leads away to the SE. Brussels has an [orbital motorway](/wiki/Orbital_motorway "Orbital motorway"), numbered R0 (R-zero) and commonly referred to as the [Ring](/wiki/Brussels_Ring "Brussels Ring"). It is pear-shaped, as the southern side was never built as originally conceived, owing to residents' objections.

The city centre, sometimes known as the [Pentagon](/wiki/Pentagon_(Brussels) "Pentagon (Brussels)"), is surrounded by an inner ring road, the [Small Ring](/wiki/Small_Ring,_Brussels "Small Ring, Brussels") (French: *Petite Ceinture*, Dutch: *Kleine Ring*), a sequence of boulevards formally numbered R20. These were built upon the site of the [second set of city walls](/wiki/Second_walls_of_Brussels "Second walls of Brussels") following their demolition. The [Metro line 2](/wiki/Brussels_Metro_line_2 "Brussels Metro line 2") runs alongside (not actually under) much of these. Since June 2015, a number of [central boulevards](/wiki/Central_Boulevards_of_Brussels "Central Boulevards of Brussels") inside the Pentagon have become car-free, limiting transit traffic through the old city.[[334]](#cite_note-339)

On the eastern side of the region, the R21 or [Greater Ring](/wiki/Greater_ring_(Brussels) "Greater ring (Brussels)") (French: *Grande Ceinture*, Dutch: *Grote Ring*) is formed by a string of boulevards that curves round from [Laeken](/wiki/Laeken "Laeken") to [Uccle](/wiki/Uccle "Uccle"). Some *premetro* stations (see [Brussels Metro](/wiki/Brussels_Metro "Brussels Metro")) were built on that route. A little further out, a stretch numbered R22 leads from Zaventem to [Saint-Job](/wiki/Saint-Job_railway_station "Saint-Job railway station").

## Security and emergency services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Security and emergency services")]

### Police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Police")]

Further information: [Law enforcement in Belgium](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Belgium "Law enforcement in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/EPP_Summit_March_2012_%2848%29.jpg/250px-EPP_Summit_March_2012_%2848%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:EPP_Summit_March_2012_(48).jpg)

Policeman in Brussels

The Brussels local police, supported by the federal police, is responsible for law enforcement in Brussels. The 19 municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region are divided into six police zones,[[335]](#cite_note-340) all bilingual (French and Dutch):

* 5339 Brussels Capital Ixelles: the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") and [Ixelles](/wiki/Ixelles "Ixelles")
* 5340 Brussels West: [Berchem-Sainte-Agathe](/wiki/Berchem-Sainte-Agathe "Berchem-Sainte-Agathe"), [Ganshoren](/wiki/Ganshoren "Ganshoren"), [Jette](/wiki/Jette "Jette"), [Koekelberg](/wiki/Koekelberg "Koekelberg") and [Molenbeek-Saint-Jean](/wiki/Molenbeek-Saint-Jean "Molenbeek-Saint-Jean")
* 5341 South: [Anderlecht](/wiki/Anderlecht "Anderlecht"), [Forest](/wiki/Forest,_Belgium "Forest, Belgium") and [Saint-Gilles](/wiki/Saint-Gilles,_Belgium "Saint-Gilles, Belgium")
* 5342 Uccle/Watermael-Boitsfort/Auderghem: [Auderghem](/wiki/Auderghem "Auderghem"), [Uccle](/wiki/Uccle "Uccle") and [Watermael-Boitsfort](/wiki/Watermael-Boitsfort "Watermael-Boitsfort")
* 5343 Montgomery: [Etterbeek](/wiki/Etterbeek "Etterbeek"), [Woluwe-Saint-Lambert](/wiki/Woluwe-Saint-Lambert "Woluwe-Saint-Lambert") and [Woluwe-Saint-Pierre](/wiki/Woluwe-Saint-Pierre "Woluwe-Saint-Pierre")
* 5344 Polbruno: [Evere](/wiki/Evere "Evere"), [Saint-Josse-ten-Noode](/wiki/Saint-Josse-ten-Noode "Saint-Josse-ten-Noode") and [Schaerbeek](/wiki/Schaerbeek "Schaerbeek")

### Fire department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Fire department")]

The Brussels Fire and Emergency Medical Care Service, commonly known by its acronym SIAMU (DBDMH), operates in the 19 municipalities of Brussels.[[336]](#cite_note-341) It is a class X fire department and the largest [fire service](/wiki/Fire_service "Fire service") in Belgium in terms of annual operations, equipment, and personnel. It has 9 [fire stations](/wiki/Fire_station "Fire station"), spread over the entire Brussels-Capital Region, and employs about 1,000 professional [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter "Firefighter"). As well as preventing and fighting fires, SIAMU also provides emergency medical care services in Brussels via its centralised 100 number (and the single 112 emergency number for the 27 countries of the European Union). It is bilingual (French–Dutch).

## Parks and green spaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Parks and green spaces")]

Main article: [List of parks and gardens in Brussels](/wiki/List_of_parks_and_gardens_in_Brussels "List of parks and gardens in Brussels")

Brussels is one of the greenest capitals in Europe, with over 8,000 hectares of green spaces.[[337]](#cite_note-342) Vegetation cover and natural areas are higher in the outskirts, where they have limited the [peri-urbanisation](/wiki/Peri-urbanisation "Peri-urbanisation") of the capital, but they decrease sharply towards the centre of Brussels; 10% in the central [Pentagon](/wiki/Pentagon_(Brussels) "Pentagon (Brussels)"), 30% of the municipalities in the first ring, and 71% of the municipalities in the second ring are occupied by green spaces.

Many parks and gardens, both public and privately owned, are scattered throughout the city. In addition to this, the [Sonian Forest](/wiki/Sonian_Forest "Sonian Forest") is located in its southern part and stretches out over the three [Belgian regions](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium#Regions "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). As of 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit), it has been inscribed as a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"), the only Belgian component to the multinational inscription '[Primeval Beech Forests of the Carpathians and Other Regions of Europe](/wiki/Primeval_Beech_Forests_of_the_Carpathians_and_Other_Regions_of_Europe "Primeval Beech Forests of the Carpathians and Other Regions of Europe")'.[[338]](#cite_note-343)

## Twin towns – sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brussels&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Twin towns – sister cities")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in Belgium](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Belgium "List of twin towns and sister cities in Belgium")

Brussels is [twinned](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") with the following cities:[[339]](#cite_note-344)
